bondsAccessNote: Individual EM USD sovereign bonds trade over-the-counter, and a US retail investor can only realistically buy them through brokers with a real bond desk (Interactive Brokers has the widest EM inventory; Fidelity and Schwab carry a thinner, mostly SEC-registered selection). Minimums are the killer: some SEC-registered Latin sovereigns (Mexico, Brazil, Colombia) come in $1,000 pieces, but many frontier eurobonds (Nigeria, Ghana, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Egypt) are Reg S/144A issues with $200,000 minimum denominations, which locks retail out of exactly the credits this dashboard cares about. Even where you can buy, retail fills routinely cost 1-3% in spread/markup versus institutional prices, and selling before maturity in a stressed name can be much worse. For anyone below roughly mid-six figures per position, EMB (0.39% ER, ~$14.3B, penny spreads) or EMHY (0.50%, holds the high-yield/distressed sovereigns) is the honest answer: one-share minimum, daily liquidity, and diversified default risk, at the cost of never holding a single country's bond to maturity. Verified both funds actively trading as of 2026-07-07.

reformer.name: Javier Milei (president), with Luis Caputo as Economy Minister
reformer.role: President (La Libertad Avanza); Caputo runs the economic program
reformer.nextElection: General/presidential election October 2027 (Milei eligible for re-election); last national vote was the 26 Oct 2025 midterms
reformer.notes: Milei is still in power and remains the benchmark case for this thesis, but the political picture has softened since the prior asOf. A corruption scandal forced Cabinet Chief Manuel Adorni to resign in late June 2026 (photos of his wife on the presidential jet to New York triggered a March probe into real-estate purchases and luxury travel inconsistent with his salary); Milei swore in Interior Minister Diego Santilli as his fourth Cabinet chief on 1 Jul 2026, folding Interior into the Cabinet office and pivoting to negotiations with provincial governors and Congress. Milei's approval sits near 39.7% in June (AtlasIntel) with disapproval ~58%, and the share of Argentines saying corruption undermines their trust in the government rose from ~59% in April to ~71% by June. Offsetting this: the opposition remains fragmented with no consolidated challenger, and the Oct 2025 midterm landslide (LLA 40.84% vs 31.63% for Peronist Fuerza Patria, +~64 Deputies and +14 Senators, veto power) still underpins a reform mandate through 2027. On the program: labor reform passed the Senate 42-28 (Feb 2026), EU-Mercosur was ratified, three consecutive years of fiscal surplus are targeted, and on 7 Jul 2026 Caputo presented a financing program through 2027 with an explicit 'investment grade by 2031' goal. Docked more points than last quarter (93 to 88) for the corruption-driven cabinet turnover, falling approval, and a reform agenda that reporting says the Adorni saga stalled in Congress. No credible near-term threat to the program before Oct 2027, but political risk is now a live watch item rather than a non-issue.

parallelFx.notes: Gap remains effectively collapsed - a residual blue (informal) market exists but is small. bluedollar.net quotes for 8 Jul 2026: blue 1,500 buy / 1,520 sell; official 1,461.75 buy / 1,513.22 sell (retail). Rates above are midpoints; the gap is ~2.6% on the buy side and ~0.5% on the sell side, versus 50-100% historically before Milei. Most capital controls (cepo) were lifted 14 Apr 2025 with the ~$20bn IMF deal; the regime is a managed float inside a band. Since 1 Jan 2026 the band's floor/ceiling crawl monthly at the INDEC inflation rate with a 2-month lag (replacing the 1%/month crawl), paired with a pre-announced BCRA reserve-buying program. Caveat retained from prior research: PIIE (Feb 2026) warns the peso has traded near the band's upper limit, leaving little buffer against shocks. [Verifier: midpoint-vs-midpoint is ~1.5%; buy-side-only is ~2.6% - curated as 1.5%.]

countryRisk.rating: Moody's Caa1 stable (upgraded from Caa3, 17 Jul 2025 - NO further Moody's action yet as of 8 Jul 2026, though a July upgrade out of the Caa category was widely expected); Fitch B- stable (upgraded from CCC+, 5 May 2026); S&P B- stable (upgraded two notches from CCC+, 10 Jun 2026)
countryRisk.notes: JP Morgan EMBI 'riesgo pais' fell to 408bps on 7 Jul 2026 per Infobae (Perfil corroborates 408), the lowest of the Milei era and just above the 24 Apr 2018 floor of 403bps, after Caputo presented the 2026-27 financing program; the move was a 6bps decline on the day. Real-time trackers showed roughly 412bps on 5-7 Jul and ~415bps intraday around 8-9 Jul, so 408 is the recent low rather than a static level - the trend is clearly compression (2026 range cited as ~484 low to ~637 high year-to-date; >2,000bps before Milei's 2023 election). No new rating action since the prior asOf: Moody's remains Caa1 stable (last action 17 Jul 2025) and had NOT upgraded as of 8 Jul 2026 despite analyst expectations of a July move; Fitch (B-, 5 May 2026) and S&P (B-, 10 Jun 2026) upgrades were already captured last quarter. S&P cited easing external vulnerabilities and a ~$10bn 2026 energy trade surplus from Vaca Muerta; Fitch says further upgrades hinge on sustained reserve build-up.

ARG.imfProgram: Active 48-month Extended Fund Facility of about US$20-21bn (SDR 15.267bn, 479% of quota), approved 11 April 2025. [Verifier: IMF PR 26/165 states the arrangement size as 'US$20 billion (SDR 15.267 billion)'; the SDR anchor is exact, the USD headline floats with SDR/USD - shown as a US$20-21bn range.] Second review completed by the Executive Board on 21 May 2026 together with the 2026 Article IV consultation, unlocking ~US$1bn (SDR 0.8bn) and bringing total disbursements to ~US$15.8bn (SDR 11.452bn). The Board judged implementation strong: the end-Dec 2025 net international reserves (NIR) accumulation target was missed but most other performance criteria were met and corrective measures were adopted. Notably, the IMF reported NIR was up about US$4.8bn since end-2025 on the BCRA's daily FX-purchase program, and projects NIR to rise by at least US$8bn in 2026 with central-bank FX purchases of at least US$10bn this year - a materially more constructive reserves picture than the prior entry conveyed. No third review has been completed as of 8 Jul 2026; the next review is upcoming.

[Verifier: YoY corrected from 33.6 to 33.2. The official INDEC May IPC report and multiple outlets (Infobae, iProfesional, El Cronista) state 33.2% interanual and 14.7% acumulado 2026; 33.6% was a Trading Economics figure the researcher had labeled 'cross-checked' but it is not the official print. INDEC-report value 33.2% now used; April official YoY ~32.4%.] Disinflation remains STALLED in the low-30s rather than continuing to fall. Monthly path for 2026: Jan 2.9%, Feb 2.9%, Mar 3.4% (utility/transport tariff catch-up), Apr 2.6% (revised down from a prior 2.8% entry), May 2.1% (lowest monthly since Sep 2025). Forward read: City of Buenos Aires (CABA) June inflation printed 1.8% m/m (32.6% YoY, 16% H1 cumulative) as a national precursor, and forecasters (C&T ~1.9%, Libertad y Progreso 1.8%, BCRA REM ~2.1%) projected national June near 1.8-2.1% - if confirmed, June would extend the easing, but the level is still ~33% YoY. PIIE warns the inflation-indexed band mechanically accommodates whatever inflation the economy produces.

ARG.fiscalNote: Still in surplus. The Treasury closed the first half of 2026 (H1) with a primary surplus of ~0.7% of GDP (per the Dinamica Economica consolidated macro report), consistent with the ~0.5%-of-GDP primary / ~0.2% financial surplus reported for Jan-Apr and following full-year fiscal surpluses in 2024 and 2025. The 2026 target is a primary surplus of ~1.2-1.6% of GDP (IMF-agreed 1.6%; budget framing cites 1.2-1.5%), which the government says would make three consecutive years of financial surplus. Quality of the adjustment is a caveat analysts now flag more loudly: with export duties and the dollar (PAIS) tax removed, the surplus leans more on domestic consumption taxes (VAT) and the real erosion of non-indexed spending, and April data showed the primary surplus down ~16.5% y/y in real terms with tax revenue down ~4% real (export-duty receipts collapsing). No deficit flip: fiscal is intact but softening at the margin, and discipline must hold through December to hit the full-year target.
developments: Cabinet Chief Manuel Adorni resigned in late June 2026 amid a corruption/illicit-enrichment probe (undisclosed real estate, cash-paid renovations, a New York trip on the presidential jet); Milei swore in Interior Minister Diego Santilli as his fourth Cabinet chief on 1 Jul 2026 and merged Interior into the Cabinet office [source: https://www.batimes.com.ar/news/argentina/santilli-sworn-in-as-cabinet-chief-as-milei-reshuffles-pack.phtml]

The Adorni scandal dented Milei politically: approval slipped to ~39.7% in June (AtlasIntel) with disapproval ~58%, and the share saying corruption undermines trust in the government rose from ~59% (April) to ~71% (June), though the opposition remains fragmented with no consolidated challenger for 2027 [source: https://thedialogue.org/analysis/why-are-mileis-approval-ratings-falling-in-argentina]

On 7 Jul 2026 Caputo presented a financing program through 2027 (new Bonar 2029 / AO29 at 6% with monthly USD coupons, no international issuance needed in the base case, 'investment grade by 2031' goal); riesgo pais fell to 408bps, just above the Apr 2018 record low [source: https://www.infobae.com/economia/2026/07/07/el-riesgo-pais-toco-el-nivel-mas-bajo-en-la-era-milei-tras-la-presentacion-del-programa-financiero/]

IMF Board completed the second EFF review on 21 May 2026, disbursing ~US$1bn (total ~US$15.8bn of ~US$21bn); implementation judged strong; end-Dec NIR target was missed but NIR is up ~US$4.8bn since end-2025 and projected to rise at least US$8bn in 2026 [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/05/21/pr26165-argentina-imf-completes-2nd-rev-of-extended-arr-under-eff-concludes-2026-aiv-consultation]

S&P upgraded Argentina two notches to B- (stable) from CCC+ on 10 Jun 2026, citing easing vulnerabilities and a projected ~US$10bn 2026 energy trade surplus from Vaca Muerta; Moody's had NOT followed with an upgrade as of 8 Jul 2026 (still Caa1 stable) despite market expectations of a July move [source: https://countryeconomy.com/ratings/argentina]

The Treasury closed H1 2026 with a primary surplus of ~0.7% of GDP; analysts note the adjustment now leans more on consumption taxes and non-indexed-spending erosion after export duties and the dollar tax were removed [source: https://dinamicaeconomica.wordpress.com/2026/07/01/consolidated-macroeconomic-report/]

CABA (City of Buenos Aires) June 2026 inflation printed 1.8% m/m (32.6% YoY), a precursor to the national INDEC June figure due mid-July; the latest official national print remains May 2026 at 2.1% m/m [source: https://www.cronista.com/economia-politica/se-conoce-la-inflacion-de-junio-caba-difunde-el-ipc-del-mes/]

Milei on 8 Jul 2026 proposed a U.S.-style government-shutdown mechanism to halt executive spending once the approved budget is exhausted, reinforcing the fiscal-discipline signal [source: https://www.upi.com/Top_News/World-News/2026/07/08/latam-argentina-government-shutdown-mechanism-proposed/7021783526744/]
killCriteriaWatch: None of the four kill criteria is tripped as of 2026-07-08, but political risk has moved from dormant to a live watch item. Reformer in power: intact (Milei governing, no election until Oct 2027) BUT weakened by a corruption scandal that cost Cabinet Chief Adorni his job (resigned late June, Santilli sworn in 1 Jul), pushed approval to ~39.7% and, per reporting, stalled the reform agenda in Congress. Not tripped (opposition fragmented, no challenger), but this is now the primary thing to watch into the Oct 2027 cycle.
Country risk: SAFE and improving - riesgo pais 408bps on 7 Jul (near the Apr 2018 low), trend compressing; no adverse rating action (Moody's still Caa1 stable, Fitch/S&P at B-).

Parallel FX gap: SAFE - blue-vs-official gap ~0.5-2.6% (midpoint ~1.5%); the crawling-band regime is holding. Early-warning caveat retained: PIIE flags the peso near the band ceiling and an inflation-indexed band with a weak nominal anchor, so an adverse shock could reopen the gap.

Fiscal / budget flip: NOT tripped - H1 2026 primary surplus ~0.7% of GDP, no deficit reversal. Softening at the margin (April primary surplus -16.5% y/y real, tax revenue -4% real, export duties collapsing) and adjustment quality is more regressive, so the 1.6% full-year target requires discipline through December. Monitor monthly Treasury results.

Supporting watch item - reserves: end-Dec 2025 IMF NIR target was missed (corrective measures adopted), but NIR is now up ~US$4.8bn since end-2025 and the IMF projects +US$8bn in 2026 with =>US$10bn of BCRA FX purchases - a better trajectory than last quarter. A stall in reserve accumulation would precede any FX/gap trouble.

reformer.name: Daniel Noboa (president); Sariha Moya (economy/finance chief)
reformer.role: Noboa: President since Nov 2023, re-elected April 2025. Moya: Minister of Economy and Finance since 16 Apr 2025; since the June 2026 cabinet consolidation she heads the merged Ministry of Economic Development and Production (finance + agriculture + production).
reformer.nextElection: Sectional/local elections (mayors, prefects, councillors) were MOVED FORWARD to 29 Nov 2026 - the CNE brought them 77 days earlier from 14 Feb 2027 on 27 Mar 2026, officially citing El Nino weather risk. Presidential/legislative general election remains due Feb 2029 (Noboa's term runs to May 2029). The Nov 2026 vote is now the near-term political test, contested amid opposition bans.
reformer.notes: Serious, IMF-anchored orthodox program rather than Milei-style shock therapy: diesel subsidy removed Sept 2025 ($1.80->$2.80/gal, ~$1.1bn/yr savings) despite a 31-day indigenous-led national strike; tax-expenditure streamlining and corrective fiscal package after late-2025 slippage; return to bond markets Jan 2026. Score cut this refresh (68 -> 58) on a marked democratic-quality deterioration since the prior asOf: after losing the Nov 2025 referendum 61.7%, the government moved the Nov 2026 local elections forward and barred its main opponents - the Contentious-Electoral Tribunal (TCE) suspended Rafael Correa's Revolucion Ciudadana for nine months (March 2026) on a corruption allegation, and Union/Unidad Popular and Construye face cancellation for allegedly failing minimum-membership thresholds. Multiple constitutional challenges to the rescheduling are pending before the Constitutional Court. Independent watchdogs (CEPR, Democratic Erosion) describe an accelerating authoritarian turn. Separately Noboa declared a new 60-day state of emergency in June 2026 across 10 provinces (879 homicides 1 May-12 Jun 2026) with warrantless home entry. The economic reform program is intact and Noboa is secure in office to 2029, but rule-of-law and governability risk is now materially higher than at the prior asOf.

parallelFx.notes: Not applicable: Ecuador has been fully dollarized since 9 Jan 2000 (sucre abolished under Mahuad); the US dollar is legal tender, so there is no own-currency exchange rate and no parallel FX market. Polling cited at the 25-year anniversary (Jan 2025) shows ~90% public support for dollarization; de-dollarization is not on any major party's agenda. Rates set to null because no ECU exchange rate exists.

countryRisk.notes: [Verifier: MATERIAL CORRECTION. The researcher reported 378 bps 'falling' and called 378 (15 Jun) the latest verified reading. Independent BCE-series reporting (El Universo, Primicias, eldiario.ec) shows the spread BOTTOMED at 378 on 15 Jun 2026 and then REVERSED UPWARD: 391 on 16 Jun, 396 on 17 Jun, 402 on 18-21 Jun, 403 on 22 Jun, and 411 on 23 Jun 2026. spreadBps updated 378 -> 411 and trend 'falling' -> 'rising'. A July 2026 print was not findable this run; 411 on 23 Jun is the latest verified reading (~2.5 weeks old).] Context: the spread began 2026 at 492 (down ~81 bps YTD to the 23 Jun 411 print, or ~114 bps to the 15 Jun 378 trough) vs 2,016 bps when Noboa took office in Nov 2023. Compression drivers: IMF program performance, Jan 2026 $4bn market return with $3bn buyback, and record reserves (USD 13,283.9m on 15 May 2026, up from $11.34bn in Jan 2026). The reversal off the mid-June low coincides with the oil-tailwind reversal: Brent fell toward ~$85/bbl in June 2026 (down from ~$110-114 in the spring) after the US and Iran signed a 18 Jun 2026 memorandum reopening the Strait of Hormuz; as a crude exporter Ecuador loses fiscal revenue when oil falls, so both the spread and the risk mix have turned less favourable.

ECU.imfProgram: 48-month Extended Fund Facility of SDR 3.75bn (~US$5bn), approved May 2024 and augmented July 2025. Fifth review completed by the Executive Board on 22 Apr 2026 (staff-level agreement 31 Mar 2026), unlocking an immediate disbursement of SDR 280.5m (~US$394m) and bringing cumulative disbursements to SDR 2.7bn (~US$3.7bn); the review included waivers of nonobservance of performance criteria after the late-2025 fiscal overshoot, with corrective revenue/spending measures enacted. IMF flagged growth exceeding expectations, low inflation, sizable current-account surpluses and record reserves. No sixth review has been announced as of this asOf.

latestInflation.notes: Latest cleanly-verified print is May 2026: 0.92% YoY, -0.62% m/m (INEC data via Trading Economics, fetched directly this run; deceleration from 2.6% YoY in April). A June 2026 release appears to exist - an INEC headline reads 'La inflacion de junio en Ecuador se ubico en -0,14%' (monthly) and one search extraction cited ~2.68% YoY / 0.94% accumulated - but I could NOT get a clean primary fetch (the INEC and Primicias pages returned cached/template content from prior years) and the extracted June figures conflicted across sources, so June is left unconfirmed and May is retained as the verified value. Inflation is structurally low-to-negative under dollarization (2025 average ~1.5%); recent months show monthly deflation. Inflation is NOT the problem in this country - the thesis leg here is fiscal + spreads and, increasingly, political/rule-of-law risk, not disinflation.

ECU.fiscalNote: Still in deficit but consolidating under the IMF program, with a late-2025 slippage corrected at the 5th review. IMF 5th review (Apr 2026): NFPS overall deficit was 2.9% of GDP in 2025, overshooting the 4th-review projection by 1.7pp of GDP; corrective measures (streamlined tax expenditures, revenue strengthening, spending efficiency) enacted, targeting ~1.6% of GDP of additional non-oil primary consolidation in 2026. Moody's (Jan 2026): central government deficit ~2.3% of GDP in 2025 vs 2.8% in 2024, forecast ~1.8% in 2026. Perimeters differ: Rio Times/Ecuador coverage puts the central-government cash deficit at ~$5.3bn (~4% of GDP) in 2025, up ~71% from ~$3.1bn in 2024, driven by an ~11% spending rise and a ~15% drop in oil revenue. Reserves at a record USD 13,283.9m as of 15 May 2026 (up from $11.34bn in Jan 2026 and $8.65bn at end-2024). NEW RISK this refresh: Brent fell to ~$85/bbl in June 2026 (from ~$110-114 in spring) after the 18 Jun US-Iran Strait-of-Hormuz deal; because 2025's deficit was already worsened by falling oil revenue, a sustained ~$85 Brent is the main channel that could reopen fiscal slippage in 2026-2027.
developments: Jan 26, 2026: Ecuador returned to international bond markets for the first time since 2019, selling $4bn on an $18bn, 4.5x-oversubscribed book, and used ~$3bn to buy back the 2030s/2035s, cutting debt service ~$1.2bn through 2028 [source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-27/ecuador-credit-upgraded-by-moody-s-after-4-billion-bond-sale]

Jan 26, 2026: Moody's upgraded Ecuador two notches to Caa1 (stable) from Caa3 after the $4bn bond sale, citing improving fiscal standing under the IMF program and restored market access [source: https://countryeconomy.com/ratings/ecuador]

Feb 25, 2026: Fitch upgraded Ecuador to B- (stable) from CCC+, citing reduced financing needs, diversified credit sources, internal-demand recovery and record non-oil exports [source: https://www.eloriente.com/articulo/fitch-ratings-mejora-la-calificacion-de-ecuador-y-mantiene-perspectiva-estable/55614]

Mar 27, 2026: The CNE moved Ecuador's sectional/local elections FORWARD 77 days, from 14 Feb 2027 to 29 Nov 2026, officially citing El Nino weather risk; opposition parties (PSC, Revolucion Ciudadana) challenged the rescheduling, with constitutional actions pending before the Constitutional Court [source: https://thecuencadispatch.com/legal-storm-builds-over-ecuadors-moved-up-local-elections/]

Apr 22, 2026: IMF Executive Board completed the fifth EFF review, disbursing SDR 280.5m (~$394m) and lifting cumulative disbursements to SDR 2.7bn (~$3.7bn), with waivers for the late-2025 fiscal overshoot (2025 NFPS deficit 2.9% of GDP) against enacted corrective measures [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/04/22/pr26126-ecuador-the-imf-concludes-5th-review-of-eff-arrangement]

killCriteriaWatch: Reformer losing power / rule-of-law erosion - CLOSEST TO TRIPPING (deteriorated since prior asOf): Noboa is secure in office until 2029, but the government moved the local elections forward to 29 Nov 2026 and barred its main opponents (Revolucion Ciudadana suspended nine months March 2026; Union/Unidad Popular and Construye facing cancellation), drawing 'blow to democracy' charges and constitutional challenges. Combined with the Nov 2025 referendum loss, a fresh 60-day state of emergency (June 2026) with warrantless searches, record homicides, and documented pressure on the Constitutional Court, this is an accelerating governability/rule-of-law problem. The economic program is not (yet) reversing, but the democratic-quality signal is the reason the reformer score was cut 68 -> 58 this refresh.

Budget flipping back to deficit - WATCHED, newly pressured: Ecuador never reached surplus; the 2025 NFPS deficit of 2.9% of GDP overshot the IMF projection by 1.7pp, forcing waivers at the 5th review. The 2025 central-government cash deficit widened to ~$5.3bn (~4% of GDP) partly on a ~15% oil-revenue drop. NEW: Brent fell to ~$85/bbl in June 2026 (from ~$110-114) after the US-Iran Strait-of-Hormuz deal; as a crude exporter Ecuador is fiscally exposed to that decline, so the main channel for renewed slippage (a weaker oil price) has now partly materialised - the single most important thing to watch next.

Country risk blowing out - NOT tripping but the downtrend has stalled: the spread hit a 12-year low of 378 bps on 15 Jun 2026 then rose back to 411 bps by 23 Jun 2026 (latest verified print) as oil fell; still far below the ~2,016 of Nov 2023, and ratings are unchanged (Moody's Caa1 / Fitch B- / S&P B-, all stable). Vulnerabilities are oil-price dependence, heavy 2026-2028 debt service, and the political-risk overhang; the June spread reversal is the first sign of the oil channel feeding through. [Verifier: updated from '378 and falling' to reflect the confirmed 15-23 Jun 2026 rebound to 411.]

Forced devaluation / parallel gap reopening - NOT APPLICABLE: fully dollarized since 2000 with ~90% public support; no own currency exists to devalue. The dollarization-specific tail risk is a liquidity/deposit crisis rather than an FX gap, and record reserves (USD 13.28bn on 15 May 2026) make that remote near-term.

reformer.nextElection: Presidential election due 2030 (Sisi's third term ends 2030; constitution currently bars re-election, but analysts report the new loyalist parliament could pass amendments extending his rule). reformer.notes: Confirmed in power as of early July 2026 (Sisi met PM Madbouly and the industry minister on July 6, 2026 to review the National Industrial Strategy 2026-2030, and received 17 new ambassadors' credentials on July 1). This is IMF-anchored orthodoxy driven by necessity, not Milei-grade ideological shock therapy. Delivered: March 2024 float and flexible FX regime maintained (the IMF and Fitch both credit the flexible exchange rate with cushioning the 2026 war shock), fuel price hikes (+17% in March 2026 even mid-war), electricity tariff hikes, a primary surplus that has exceeded target, and a 2026 law signed by Sisi repealing Article 27 barriers to selling state companies. As of the June 29 / July 1, 2026 IMF staff-level agreement, real GDP growth reached 5% in Q3 FY25/26 (5.2% over the first three quarters) and the primary balance and tax-revenue targets were surpassed by end-March 2026. Constraint: IMF still calls structural reform progress 'uneven' and says accelerating the State Ownership Policy and shrinking the state's (and military's) economic footprint remains essential. Political risk to program continuity is low (authoritarian control, loyalist parliament with Nation's Future holding 227/568 seats); the risk is reform depth, not reversal. Score 55 = orthodox and durable but constrained by the state/military economy. [Verifier: independently re-confirmed Sisi in power and active as president in early July 2026 (inaugurated the State Strategic Command Headquarters July 4, received 17 ambassadors' credentials July 1, met PM Madbouly and the industry minister on the National Industrial Strategy 2026-2030 July 6). Parliamentary final results (Nation's Future 227/568, turnout 32.41%, announced Jan 10 2026) independently confirmed. No coup, resignation or leadership change found.]

EGY.imfProgram: Active and progressing. 46-month Extended Fund Facility approved Dec 16, 2022, extended through Dec 15, 2026, plus a Resilience and Sustainability Facility. The IMF Executive Board completed the combined 5th and 6th EFF reviews and the 1st RSF review on Feb 26, 2026, unlocking ~US$2.3bn (~$2bn EFF + $273m RSF), taking cumulative drawings to ~US$5.2bn. On June 29, 2026 IMF staff and the authorities reached a NEW staff-level agreement on the 7th EFF review and 2nd RSF review, which - once approved by the Executive Board - would disburse ~US$1.64bn (SDR 1.11bn / ~$1.5bn EFF plus SDR 100m / ~$136m RSF) and take total program disbursements to about US$7.2bn. Staff reported real GDP growth of 5% in Q3 FY25/26 (5.2% over the first three quarters), primary-balance and tax-revenue targets surpassed by end-March 2026, and the war impact 'relatively contained' thanks to fuel/electricity price adjustments and spending reprioritization. No waiver or off-track signal was flagged in the June statement; the IMF continues to call structural reform 'uneven' and urges faster State Ownership Policy implementation and private-sector participation.

The easing was driven by fading fuel passthrough - transport inflation slowed to 24.7% in May from 29.2% in April. Trend is falling from the March 2026 war spike (~15.2%) but still above the January 2026 trough of 11.9% (per IMF); the earlier re-acceleration was war-related fuel/electricity/transport hikes, not FX passthrough. CBE held its policy rate at 19% on May 21, 2026, warning fiscal-adjustment measures and energy prices could slow disinflation toward its Q4 2026 target. The IMF's June statement noted headline inflation had ticked up recently. Note: primary CBE and CAPMAS pages (cbe.org.eg CPI release, capmas.gov.eg) returned 403/rejection this run, so the May figures are corroborated via CAPMAS-sourced press reporting and TradingEconomics rather than a directly fetched official PDF.

EGY.fiscalNote: Primary surplus improving, overall balance still in deficit. Per the IMF's June 29, 2026 statement, the primary-balance and tax-revenue targets were surpassed by end-March 2026 on robust revenue collection and spending within budget. First 9 months of FY2025/26 (Jul 2025-Mar 2026): primary surplus EGP 748.8bn = 3.5% of GDP (vs 2.4% a year earlier), while the overall budget deficit was 5.2% of GDP over the same 9 months (war costs, energy subsidies, heavy interest bill). FY2025/26 targets: primary surplus 4% of GDP (EGP 795bn), overall deficit ~7.3% of GDP on a glidepath to ~5.5% in FY26/27. [Verifier: independently confirmed via the IMF/Arab News coverage of the June 29 2026 SLA that the primary surplus is now tracking ABOVE the original 4% budget target - the IMF projects a primary surplus of 4.8% of GDP in FY2025/26 rising to 5.0% in FY2026/27, with a FY2026/27 growth target of 5.4%; the 4% figure above is the original budget target, not the latest IMF projection.] Moody's: interest payments peak in FY2026 at ~63% of general government revenue (~11% of GDP); S&P: general government debt falling to ~89% of GDP by June 2026 from a 94% peak in 2023; gross financing needs still >40% of GDP. The 2026-27 budget introduced new tax, customs and investment incentives while protecting health/education and export/entrepreneurship programs.
developments: IMF staff and Egypt reached a NEW staff-level agreement on the 7th EFF review and 2nd RSF review on June 29, 2026, unlocking ~$1.64bn (SDR 1.11bn / ~$1.5bn EFF + SDR 100m / ~$136m RSF) once the Board approves, taking total program disbursements to ~$7.2bn; staff reported 5% GDP growth in Q3 FY25/26, primary-balance and tax-revenue targets surpassed, and the war impact 'relatively contained' [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/06/29/pr26231-egypt-imf-staff-reaches-sla-7th-review-eff-2nd-review-rsf]

The IMF Executive Board had earlier completed the 5th and 6th EFF reviews and 1st RSF review on Feb 26, 2026, unlocking ~$2.3bn and extending the EFF through Dec 15, 2026; growth was revised up to 4.4% for FY2024/25 and structural reform progress was called 'uneven' [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/02/26/pr-26064-egypt-imf-completes-5th-and-6th-revs-under-ext-arrange-under-eff-and-1st-rev-under-rsa]

Fitch affirmed Egypt at B with a stable outlook on May 17, 2026, crediting the flexible exchange rate with limiting the impact of foreign portfolio outflows and regional tensions, and citing reserves ~$53bn (end-April) and remittances +30% to $22bn in H1 FY25/26, while flagging weak public finances and geopolitical risk [source: https://www.egypttoday.com/Article/3/147214/Flexible-exchange-rate-supports-Egypt%E2%80%99s-stable-outlook-Fitch]

Suez Canal revenue for FY2025/26 came in at $4.67bn (EGP 230.22bn), up 23% YoY, as regional tensions and Red Sea disruption eased following the Iran-US de-escalation - a recovery in a key hard-currency channel that had slumped during the war [source: https://egyptianstreets.com/2026/06/30/suez-canal-revenue-rises-23-percent-in-the-2025-2026-fiscal-year-as-regional-tensions-ease/]

Privatization push continued: on June 28, 2026 the cabinet added four more state firms - ENPPI, Egyptian Linear Alkyl Benzene (ELAB), Petroleum Marine Services, and Maamoura for Reconstruction and Tourism - to the asset-sale program, part of the drive to hit divestment proceeds that help unlock remaining IMF tranches [source: https://enterpriseam.com/egypt/2026/04/05/egypt-looks-to-restart-privatization-push-with-five-new-egx-filings/]

Egypt regained market access in May 2026 with a $1bn 8-year USD social/sustainable bond priced at 7.6% (tightened from 8% guidance) on a book more than 5x covered - its first international USD issuance since the war began, proceeds earmarked for health and education [source: https://internationalfinance.com/markets/egypt-raises-usd-billion-dollar-denominated-social-bond-sale/]

The 2026 Iran war had hit Egypt hard in Feb-Apr 2026 (Israeli gas cut, Suez Q1 revenue -38% YoY, fuel +17%, EGP to ~54.9/USD) but the shock proved contained: by early July the pound had recovered to ~49.6/USD (+~4% over the prior month) and the IMF called the war impact 'relatively contained' [source: https://themiddleeastinsider.com/2026/04/04/iran-war-impact-egypt-economy-5-numbers-2026/]

CBE held its policy rate at 19% on May 21, 2026 as inflation re-accelerated earlier in the year and then began easing again; May urban inflation slowed to 14.6% YoY (from 14.9% April), the lowest since February [source: https://sis.gov.eg/en/media-center/news/cbe-keeps-key-interest-rates-unchanged-as-inflation-pressures-persist/]

Parliamentary elections concluded with final results Jan 10, 2026: pro-Sisi Nation's Future won 227 of 568 elected seats on 32.4% turnout, with results annulled in dozens of districts over irregularities - cementing a loyalist legislature that analysts say could amend the constitution to extend Sisi's rule beyond 2030 [source: https://www.idea.int/democracytracker/report/egypt/november-2025]

Not currently tripped, but war/energy-import flows remain the swing factor to watch.

Country risk blowing out - spiked in Q1 2026 (Egypt detracted 18bps from EMBI GD returns; ~$10bn outflows) but recompressed by May-June (7.6% new-issue yield, 5x book, Fitch B/stable affirmed May 17). No live spread was fetchable this run so the level is null, but every fetched signal (reserves rising to ~$59.2bn, Suez revenue +23% FY25/26, new IMF SLA) points to falling risk. Not tripped.

Budget flipping back to deficit - the OVERALL balance has always been in deficit (5.2% of GDP in 9M FY25/26); the thesis-relevant metric, the primary surplus, is intact and improving (3.5% of GDP, and the IMF says the primary-balance target was surpassed by end-March 2026). Slippage risks remain energy subsidies and an interest bill peaking near ~63% of revenue in FY2026. Not tripped on the primary metric.

Reformer losing power - NOT close to tripping: Sisi is confirmed in power (active as president July 6, 2026), entrenched with a loyalist parliament through 2030, and the IMF program runs to Dec 2026 with a fresh 7th-review SLA in hand. The real thesis risk is reform shallowness (state/military footprint barely shrinking, IMF still calls progress 'uneven'), i.e., the re-rating stalls rather than reverses.

reformer.notes: Still in power and unchallenged electorally: NEBE's final results (announced 21 Jun 2026) gave Abiy's Prosperity Party 438 seats, with EZEMA 13, National Movement of Amhara 6, Medrek 3, Freedom and Equality 3, Coalition for Unity and Democracy 2; reported turnout 94%, but no voting at all in the entire Tigray Region. Program remains serious and orthodox: July 2024 FX float, 15% policy rate with a 24% credit-growth cap, IMF ECF with all quantitative performance criteria met through five reviews, banking sector opened to foreign banks (June 2025 directive), Ethiopian Securities Exchange launched Jan 2025, Eurobond restructuring near done. But it is gradualist state-led reform, not Milei-grade shock therapy: parallel FX market persists, credit caps still in place, SOE strategy is reform-not-privatisation. Score cut from 65 to 62 because the conflict tail-risk has moved from theoretical to active: TPLF-federal clashes erupted in late Jan 2026 (Tigrayan forces briefly captured Alamata and Korem), the TPLF restored the suspended Tigray regional government on 20 Apr 2026 in a repudiation of the 2022 Pretoria deal, and by mid-2026 leaders in Ethiopia, Eritrea and Tigray were all publicly warning that a return to war may be imminent (Abiy told parliament in early July 2026 that Ethiopia pursues sea access peacefully but underscored its sovereign right and defensive capacity, per Addis Standard). Political control is strong; the derailment risk is a northern war, not the ballot box. [Verifier: removed the Isaias Afwerki 'plan of a crazy person' quote 'on 19 Jul' - that statement was made 19 July 2025 (reported in The Africa Report's 5 Nov 2025 'Summer of escalation' piece), NOT July 2026; it is a stale 2025 event and, as written with a '19 Jul' date, would be a future-dated event relative to this 8 Jul 2026 snapshot. It was also misattributed to the Atlantic Council source (22 May 2026), which does not contain the quote. parallelFx.notes: Birr was floated/unified July 2024 under the IMF program, but a parallel market never fully closed and the birr keeps depreciating (interbank USD/ETB ~161.16 on 9 Jul 2026 per Trading Economics, down ~17% over the past year). Readings are noisy and source-dependent: as of June-July 2026 forex bureaus were quoting ~173 birr (a narrow ~2.8% premium to the parallel), private banks averaged ~160 (an ~11% premium to parallel), while the parallel/black-market rate sat around 174-177; some desks still describe the parallel as running 15-20% above the official bank rate. The officialRate here is the ~161.2 interbank print and parallelRate the ~175 street rate, implying roughly a 9% gap, but treat the true premium as an 8-20% RANGE depending on which 'official' benchmark you use - all of it well above the NBE's 5-8% target. Gap history: blew out to ~27% (137 vs 174) in Aug 2025, nearly converged in Nov 2025, re-widened in early 2026; NBE ran special $100m FX auctions (e.g. 9 Jun 2026) to defend the birr and pull flows into formal channels.

countryRisk.notes: No clean current EMBI spread is fetchable: Ethiopia remains in default on its sole ~$1bn 6.625% Eurobond (missed Dec 2023 coupon), so a headline spread is not meaningful; stale reference: EMBI/CDS ~6,000bps at the Dec 2023 default. spreadBps therefore set to null (never invented). Market pricing signals sharply falling risk: the defaulted bond traded around 107.6 cents on the dollar in late June 2026, its highest since January, after the new preliminary restructuring deal. Restructuring path: a Jan 2026 agreement-in-principle (15% haircut) was rejected by the Official Creditor Committee on 30 Jan 2026 and formal talks collapsed on 31 May 2026, before a new preliminary deal on 29-30 June 2026 (new $880m bond = 12% haircut, 6.15% coupon, amortizing Jul 2026-Jul 2029, $99.375m past-due interest paid at settlement plus a 0.5% consent fee, plus a detachable New Money Warrant exercisable from Jul 2028). The deal was struck with an Ad Hoc Committee holding ~45% of the notes and is reported to be IMF-DSA-consistent; Ethiopia plans to implement it via an exchange offer 'in the coming months.' Ratings should cure upward once the exchange completes; trend = risk falling, but default status not yet exited.

ETH.imfProgram: Active 48-month ECF, SDR 2.556bn (~US$3.4bn, 850% of quota), approved 29 July 2024 supporting the HGER agenda. Fifth review completed 1 July 2026 (staff-level agreement reached 3 June 2026): US$464m (SDR 342.05m) disbursed, bringing total disbursements to ~US$2.647bn; all quantitative performance criteria and most indicative targets met; ~US$200m rephased forward to cushion the Middle East war fuel-price shock. Fourth review had been completed 16 January 2026 (~US$261m). No sixth review has been completed or scheduled yet as of this refresh. In its July 2026 messaging the IMF urged the NBE to keep a tight monetary stance and to be ready to tighten further if inflation pressures re-emerge, and pushed for deeper FX-market reform (expanding the interbank FX market, easing selected exchange restrictions).

latestInflation.notes: Trading Economics/CSA: May 2026 CPI +13.4% YoY (a ten-month high) and +1.7% m/m, up from 11.7% YoY in April (which was +2.3% m/m) and 9.4% in March - the June 2026 print had not yet been released as of this 8 Jul 2026 refresh, so May remains the latest available reading. Re-acceleration is driven by food (~15% YoY) and Middle East war oil-price passthrough. Single-digit inflation had been reached briefly (Feb 2026 ~9.7%) but has climbed for three straight months since. The IMF (Jul 2026) says a tight stance remains appropriate; the NBE has held the policy rate at 15% and kept the 24% credit-growth cap.

ETH.fiscalNote: Modest deficits, consolidating on revenue but budgeted to widen slightly: the IMF 5th review (Jul 2026) cited rising tax revenue and foreign-exchange reserves and strong macro performance; prior review figures had revenue mobilization near 10.8% of GDP and public debt around 45.3% of GDP in 2025/26. Parliament ratified a 2.34 trillion birr (~$14.8bn) FY2026/27 budget in early July 2026 - spending up ~21% YoY with a deficit of 308.6bn birr = 2.36% of GDP (~1.4% excluding 214bn birr amortization), financed mostly by domestic bonds/T-bills. Not a surplus story like Argentina - a small, mostly domestically financed deficit with strong revenue growth. Watch the FY2026/27 spending jump against any war-driven security outlays.
developments: IMF completed the 5th ECF review on 1 Jul 2026: $464m disbursed (total ~$2.647bn), all quantitative performance criteria and most indicative targets met, ~$200m brought forward to cushion the Middle East war fuel shock; IMF urged the NBE to be ready to tighten further if inflation re-accelerates [source: https://www.stockmarket.et/imf-approves-464-million-disbursement-for-ethiopia-brings-total-ecf-support-to-2-65-billion/]

Birr kept depreciating (interbank ~161 ETB/USD on 9 Jul 2026, ~-17% over the year) while the parallel market persisted at ~174-177 birr; forex bureaus quoted ~173 and private banks ~160, and NBE ran special $100m FX auctions (e.g. 9 Jun 2026) to defend the birr [source: https://tradingeconomics.com/ethiopia/currency]
killCriteriaWatch: Reformer losing power via war - MOVED CLOSER THIS REFRESH: not an electoral risk (438-seat win, next vote ~2031), but the Ethiopia-Eritrea-Tigray conflict has gone from 'powder keg' theory to active fact - TPLF-federal clashes in Jan-Feb 2026 with towns changing hands, TPLF restoring the Tigray government on 20 Apr 2026, and Ethiopian/Eritrean/Tigrayan leaders all warning through 2025-2026 that war may be imminent. A northern war is the realistic path to program derailment via fiscal blowout, FX pressure and donor pullback.

Parallel FX gap reopening - CHRONIC AND NEAR THE LINE: the premium is an 8-20% range depending on benchmark (interbank ~161 vs parallel ~175 = ~9%; some desks say 15-20%), vs an NBE 5-8% target; the birr is still depreciating and NBE needs repeated $100m auctions to hold it. It hit ~27% in Aug 2025, so the distortion is structural, not resolved.

Inflation re-acceleration: 13.4% YoY in May 2026, a ten-month high and rising for three straight months (food + Middle East war oil shock) - the disinflation thesis is wobbling; NBE on hold at 15% but the IMF is explicitly urging readiness to tighten.

Country-risk blowout via restructuring failure: two failed deal rounds in 2026 (OCC rejection 30 Jan, collapse 31 May) show real execution risk; if the 29 Jun 2026 deal fails comparability-of-treatment or the exchange offer flops, default status persists and spreads stay unquotable. The ~107.6-cent bond price says the market currently expects it to close, so this is a watch, not a trip.

Budget flipping back to deficit - partially moot: Ethiopia never ran a surplus; watch that the FY2026/27 deficit widening (2.36% of GDP, spending +21%) stays domestically financeable, especially if a northern war forces extra security spending, without reigniting money-printing inflation.

reformer.name: John Dramani Mahama (President) with Dr. Cassiel Ato Forson (Finance Minister) as operational driver
reformer.role: President since 7 Jan 2025 (NDC); Ato Forson is Finance Minister running the 'economic reset' program
reformer.nextElection: December 2028 general election; Mahama is constitutionally term-limited and has repeatedly ruled out running (reaffirmed 2025-2026, most recently rejecting a third-term push from NDC elders), so the program faces a succession handover in 2029
reformer.notes: Confirmed in power and active as of 8-9 Jul 2026 (declared 3 Jul Republic Day holiday, opened the 2026 Ghana-EU Partnership Dialogue, declared 10-11 Jul national cleanup after the 29 Jun floods that killed about 13). Orthodox IMF-anchored consolidation, not Milei-style shock therapy, but unusually credible delivery: statutory primary-surplus floor of 1.5% of GDP written into law, 2025 primary surplus outturn of 2.5-2.6% of GDP (vs 1.5% target), overall deficit cut sharply, public debt down GH-cedi 82.1bn in 2025, VAT overhaul in the 2026 budget (standard rate 21.9% to 20%, registration threshold raised to GH-cedi 750k), and a requested 36-month non-financing IMF PCI to anchor policy after the ECF ends. Political backing is strong: NDC won ~183-185 of 276 parliamentary seats in Dec 2024; both Mahama and NDC chair Asiedu Nketiah have publicly rejected any third-term/constitutional-amendment path. Deductions: heavy tailwind from record gold prices/GoldBod FX flows does part of the work (GoldBod spent ~US$16.1bn on gold purchases Jan 2025-May 2026, crediting ~41% cedi appreciation); IMF says structural reforms were 'implemented with delays'; a forex-bureau premium over the interbank rate suggests FX management rather than a fully clean float; IMF projects debt/GDP rising to ~53% by end-2026 (from 45.3% in 2025) on reprofiling and cash-basis gaps; and Mahama cannot run in 2028, creating continuity risk.

parallelFx.notes: Ghana runs a managed float, not a formal dual-rate regime, but a persistent retail premium exists: on 6 Jul 2026 the BoG interbank rate was GHS 11.38/11.40 (buy/sell) vs forex bureau rates of GHS 11.80/12.25 - a ~7.5% selling-rate premium (12.25/11.40 - 1 = 7.46%), unchanged from 3 Jul 2026 (same 11.40 interbank vs 12.25 bureau). On 1 Jul 2026 bureaus sold at 12.35 vs 11.36 interbank (~8.7% gap), so the gap has been broadly flat-to-narrowing over the first week of July after widening from ~10% in early Feb 2026 (10.95 interbank vs 12.10 bureau, flagged by Citi Newsroom). The cedi appreciated ~40-41% in 2025 (Africa's best performer, first annual gain since 1994) helped by GoldBod gold-export FX inflows, so the premium reflects rationed access at the interbank rate rather than a collapsing peg - but it remains the single clearest crack in the story. [Verifier: gap steady at ~7.5% across 3-6 Jul 2026, bureau 12.25 vs interbank 11.40.]

GHA.imfProgram: Active/completing: US$3bn, 39-month ECF approved May 2023. Fifth review completed by Executive Board 17 Dec 2025, disbursing ~US$385m. Staff-level agreement on the sixth and FINAL review reached 15 May 2026 alongside the 2026 Article IV (mission Apr 29-May 15); final tranche of more than US$318m pending Board approval, which mission chief Ruben Atoyan expects on 27 Jul 2026 (funds to release the next day), bringing total disbursements to ~US$3.2bn. As of 8-9 Jul 2026 the Board had NOT yet formally completed the sixth review. Authorities requested a successor 36-month NON-FINANCING Policy Coordination Instrument (PCI) to anchor policy post-ECF - a signal of graduation rather than dependence.

latestInflation.notes: June 2026 CPI rose 5.3% YoY (m/m +0.2%), up from 3.7% in May and the THIRD consecutive monthly rise, driven by non-food (6.3%) - transport, housing, education - while food rose to 3.9% (from 3.3%). Highest reading since Dec 2025 but still dramatically below June 2025 (13.7%) and below BoG's 6-10% medium-term band. The trough was ~3.2-3.3% in Feb-Mar 2026 (lowest since 1999) after 13 straight months of disinflation from ~23.8% end-2024. Watch: BoG cut 400bps in H1 2026 (18% to 15.5% Jan 28; to 14% Mar 18) then held at 14% in May as pressure rebuilt.

GHA.fiscalNote: 2025 outturn (reported Feb 2026): primary SURPLUS of ~2.5-2.6% of GDP on commitment basis (vs 1.5% program target) and a sharply reduced overall deficit; IMF confirmed the primary surplus overperformed the program target. Public debt fell GH-cedi 82.1bn in 2025 (GH-cedi 726.7bn to ~GH-cedi 630-641bn), with debt/GDP at 45.3% in 2025 (down from 61.8% in 2024). Caveats now clearer: the IMF projects debt/GDP RISING to ~53.0% by end-2026 (then easing to ~50.7% in 2027) on post-restructuring reprofiling and financing needs, and analysts flag a large gap between the commitment-basis primary surplus and a cash-basis deficit (~4.0% of GDP), i.e. heavy interest obligations and crowding-out risk if domestic borrowing surges. A primary surplus of at least 1.5% of GDP is now a statutory requirement; the 2026 budget targets continued consolidation funded by VAT simplification and spending caps. Trajectory: consolidation holding and best fiscal position in over a decade, but the 2026 debt-ratio uptick and cash-basis gap are the honest asterisks.

killCriteriaWatch: Parallel gap reopening - CLOSEST TO WATCHING, NOT TRIPPING: forex-bureau USD selling rates (GHS 12.25) run ~7.5% above the BoG interbank rate (~11.40); the gap widened from Feb 2026 but has held flat-to-narrowing in early July, suggesting the interbank rate is below market-clearing and the 2025 cedi appreciation is partly administered via gold-export FX flows rather than a fully clean float

Debt ratio and cash-basis fiscal gap - NEW WATCH: IMF projects debt/GDP rising to ~53% by end-2026 (from 45.3%) and a cash-basis deficit near 4.0% of GDP despite the commitment-basis primary surplus; crowding-out risk if 2026 domestic borrowing surges - a real caveat, though driven by reprofiling not a discipline breakdown

Program anchor weakening - WATCH: the $3bn ECF ends mid-2026 (final review pending Board approval ~27 Jul) and the successor PCI is non-financing; IMF already notes structural reforms 'implemented with delays'; discipline is untested without Fund money on the line

reformer.name: William Ruto (President), with John Mbadi (Treasury Cabinet Secretary)
reformer.role: President drives the program; Mbadi (ODM, in cabinet since Aug 2024) runs fiscal policy
reformer.nextElection: 10 August 2027 general election (~13 months away); Ruto seeking a second and final term
reformer.notes: Still in power as of 2026-07-08. Orthodox, IMF-aligned direction but heavily constrained - nothing like shock therapy. Real wins: Moody's/S&P/Fitch improvements, return to the Eurobond market (Feb 2026, $2.25bn), reserves rebuilt (now ~$14.0bn / 6 months cover after a $750m World Bank disbursement in early July 2026), single-digit inflation. But the deficit-killing leg keeps slipping: prior $3.6bn IMF program expired April 2025 with ~$850m undisbursed after missed revenue/deficit targets (ninth review abandoned March 2025); FY25/26 deficit was revised UP to ~6% of GDP on a KSh115bn revenue shortfall; Finance Act 2026 (signed June 23, 2026) was deliberately modest ('no new taxes') because the 2024 Gen Z protests broke the government's tax-raising capacity. The currency-freeing leg is arguably going backwards: CBK holds USD/KES pinned at ~129 via heavy dollar sales, which the IMF has flagged as 'excessively stable'. Ruto is unpopular but favored to win 2027 against a divided opposition; deadly protests (Saba Saba anniversary shut down Nairobi again on July 7, 2026; 250+ killed in youth-led protests since 2024 per WSWS citing rights groups) are the main political risk. Score 39 (nudged up one point from 38 on the reserves rebuild): credible orthodox intent, weak execution and mandate, managed FX still contradicts the thesis.

parallelFx.notes: No organized parallel/black market; USD/KES 129.25 on 2026-07-08 (Trading Economics; unchanged, +0.12% over the past month, -0.04% over 12 months). BUT the rate is de facto managed, not free: it has sat in a ~129.2-129.9 band for ~16-17 months; the IMF publicly flagged the shilling as 'excessively stable'. Notable change since the prior asOf: reserves REBOUNDED from the April 2026 drawdown - CBK reserves rose $874m to $14.047bn (6 months import cover) in the week to July 2, 2026, driven mainly by a $750m World Bank governance/PFM/social-protection disbursement, versus $13.173bn the prior week and the ~$13.3bn April 9, 2026 trough. That eases the acute reserve-defense stress flagged last week, but the setup still resembles a soft peg: Treasury claimed (Nov 2025) the shilling is UNDERVALUED (fair value ~118 if freed), while Oxford Economics, Standard Chartered and Citigroup saw a managed slide to ~134 by end-2026. Gap is 0 today; the risk is a step-devaluation or messy exit from the soft peg rather than a live parallel market.

Sourced yield levels (week ended Jul 3, 2026, Mwango Capital weekly): KENINT 2027 6.115%, KENINT 2032 7.592%, KENINT 2034 7.867% - versus ~18% peak yields in Nov 2023, i.e. a massive multi-year re-rating. Kenya re-entered the Eurobond market Feb 2026 with $2.25bn ($900m 7.875% due 2034 at ~8.1% reoffer; $1.35bn 8.70% due 2039 at ~8.95%), using ~$500m to tender 2028/2032 notes. Near-term wobble: Eurobond yields rose an average ~14bps in the week ending Jul 3, 2026 (KENINT 2032 +27.9bps, 2034 +19.1bps, 2027 +1.8bps) amid the protest anniversary and post-budget jitters, so trend is flat-to-rising at the short end of the recovery rather than still falling. Moody's core caveat: debt affordability among weakest in its sovereign universe, interest >30% of revenue; IMF projects debt/GDP rising to ~71.6% end-2026 and ~72.4% end-2027.

KEN.imfProgram: None currently in place as of 2026-07-08. The 2021 $3.6bn EFF/ECF (plus RSF) expired April 2025 with the ninth review abandoned (March 2025) and ~$850m undisbursed after Kenya missed deficit/revenue targets. A new funded program has been under negotiation through 2026 (missions Jan and Feb-Mar 2026 yielded no lending deal); Mbadi said (Bloomberg, May 11, 2026) Kenya targets agreement by June/July 2026 - possibly ~KSh100bn (~$774m) - urgency raised by Iran-war commodity shocks. No staff-level agreement or Executive Board approval of a NEW program has been confirmed from a fetched source as of 2026-07-08 (the widely-circulated '$1.1bn SLA' headlines are the June 2024 seventh-review SLA of the OLD, now-expired program, not a new deal); analysts (Bloomberg, Sep 2025) had warned talks could drag past the Aug 2027 elections. The early-July $750m World Bank disbursement further reduces the near-term pressure to close an IMF deal.

latestInflation.asOf: June 2026 (KNBS CPI release; July 2026 print not due until ~end-July 2026)
latestInflation.notes: June 2026: 6.4% YoY (eased from 6.7% in May), +0.2% m/m (a sharp slowdown from +1.6% m/m in May); confirmed against the KNBS June 2026 release. Still inside CBK's 2.5-7.5% target band but in the upper half and above 2025 levels (drivers: food & non-alcoholic beverages +8.6% YoY, transport +16.1% YoY, housing/utilities +3.4% YoY - these three ~57% of the CPI weight). This is the latest available print; the July figure is not yet released. CBK held the policy rate at 8.75% on June 9, 2026 (second straight hold after 10 cuts / 425bps of easing) and next meets in August 2026; MPC panel has turned more hawkish, a small majority now sees a hike this year on Middle-East passthrough risk. Not a hyperinflation-turnaround setup: Kenya is a moderate-inflation country, YoY drifting sideways-to-up while m/m has cooled.

KEN.fiscalNote: Persistent deficit, consolidation repeatedly slipping - never near surplus. FY2025/26: revenue shortfalls (KSh115.3bn below target by Dec 2025) forced a supplementary budget raising spending to KSh4.533trn and widening the deficit to ~KSh1.14trn ~6% of GDP (vs ~4.8-5.1% originally planned). FY2026/27 budget (presented by Mbadi June 11, 2026): KSh4.82trn expenditure vs KSh3.631trn revenue, deficit KSh1.15trn = 5.5% of GDP, financed mostly domestically (4.8% of GDP domestic vs 0.6% external); Treasury projects narrowing to 3.3% by FY2028/29 - but its past multi-year targets have consistently been missed, and the Finance Act 2026 'no new taxes' stance removes the main lever for hitting them. Public debt ~68.8% of GDP and rising (IMF sees ~71.6% end-2026); interest absorbs >30% of revenue (Moody's). 2026 growth forecast cut to 5.0% from 5.3% on Middle East conflict effects.
developments: CBK foreign-exchange reserves rebounded to $14.047bn (6 months of import cover) in the week to July 2, 2026, up $874m from $13.173bn, driven mainly by a $750m World Bank disbursement for governance, PFM and social-protection reforms - reversing much of the April 2026 reserve drawdown [source: https://thekenyatimes.com/business/kenyas-foreign-exchange-reserves-reach-usd14-05-billion-enough-for-six-months-imports/]

USD/KES was unchanged at 129.25 on July 8, 2026 (+0.12% over the prior month, -0.04% over 12 months), extending a ~16-17 month soft peg near 129 that the IMF has called 'excessively stable' [source: https://tradingeconomics.com/kenya/currency]

Kenya's Eurobond curve backed up an average ~14bps in the week ended July 3, 2026 (KENINT 2032 +27.9bps to 7.592%, 2034 +19.1bps to 7.867%, 2027 +1.8bps to 6.115%) amid the protest anniversary and post-budget jitters [source: https://mwangocapital.com/the-mwango-weekly-6th-july-2026/]

Saba Saba anniversary on July 7, 2026: police locked down central Nairobi before dawn, barricaded Parliament with barbed wire and closed the Thika-Nairobi road, declaring the notified march unlawful; no confirmed 2026 death toll yet, against 250+ killed in youth-led protests since 2024 per rights groups cited in press [source: https://www.wsws.org/en/articles/2026/07/07/rbww-j07.html]

Kenya still targets a new funded IMF program (Mbadi flagged June/July 2026, ~KSh100bn/$774m, Iran-war urgency); no staff-level agreement or board approval of a new program confirmed as of July 8, 2026 [source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-11/kenya-targets-new-imf-deal-by-july-as-iran-war-risks-mount]

FY2026/27 budget presented June 11, 2026: KSh4.82trn spending, KSh3.631trn revenue, 5.5%-of-GDP deficit, 2026 growth forecast cut to 5.0% on Middle East conflict, debt seen ~68.8% of GDP [source: https://www.tuko.co.ke/politics/629248-budget-reading-2026-john-mbadi-presens-second-budget-highlighs-national-treasury-cs/]

Ruto signed the Finance Act 2026 on June 23, 2026, insisting 'no new taxes' after the 2024/25 protest backlash - protecting social peace at the cost of consolidation ambition [source: https://peopledaily.digital/news/ruto-signs-finance-bill-2026-into-law]

June 2026 inflation eased to 6.4% YoY (from 6.7% in May) with m/m slowing to +0.2% from +1.6%; CBK held the rate at 8.75% on June 9 and next meets in August 2026 with a more hawkish panel [source: https://www.knbs.or.ke/reports/consumer-price-indices-and-inflation-rates-june-2026/]
killCriteriaWatch: Forced devaluation / parallel gap reopening - STILL THE CLOSEST TO TRIPPING BUT EASED THIS WEEK: the shilling remains de facto pegged near 129 and the IMF still calls it 'excessively stable', but the acute reserve-defense stress flagged in April has partly reversed - reserves rebounded to $14.047bn / 6 months cover in the week to July 2, 2026 on a $750m World Bank inflow, versus the ~$13.3bn April trough. A step-devaluation or messy exit from the soft peg remains the single most likely near-term trigger, just with more of a buffer than a week ago.

Budget flipping back to deficit - effectively already failed/moot: Kenya never achieved balance; FY25/26 deficit widened to ~6% of GDP versus plan on revenue shortfalls, and the Finance Act 2026 deliberately avoids new taxes, so FY26/27's 5.5% target carries clear downside risk.

Reformer losing power - WATCH, not tripped: Ruto still in office and favored for the Aug 10, 2027 election against a divided opposition, but is deeply unpopular; renewed Saba Saba mobilization (Nairobi locked down July 7, 2026), 250+ protest deaths since 2024, and warnings of electoral violence keep the mandate fragile, and a new IMF deal could still slip past the election.

Country risk blowing out - NOT tripping, but momentum has stalled: ratings still B3/B/B- with market access restored at ~8.1-8.95%, yields near multi-year lows (6.1-7.9%); however the curve backed up ~14bps (up to +27.9bps at the 2032 point) in the week to Jul 3, 2026, so the trend is flat-to-rising rather than still falling.

The IMF's combined 5th+6th review rated debt-sustainability risk HIGH across all horizons, with public debt still above 100% of GDP in 2026; gross official reserves were only US$6.8bn at end-2025 (~3.1 months of imports), projected to reach ~US$8.6bn by end-2026, and the Middle East war has slowed reserve accumulation. LKA.imfProgram: 4-year EFF of SDR 2.3bn (~US$3bn) approved 20 Mar 2023, in its final year (runs to ~Mar 2027). Combined 5th+6th reviews completed by the Executive Board on 27 May 2026 (5th review had been deferred after Cyclone Ditwah), releasing SDR 508mn (~US$695mn) and bringing total purchases to SDR 1.778bn (~US$2.4bn). All end-Dec-2025 QPCs met; one waiver of non-observance was granted for a continuous PC breach after a US$2.5mn debt payment to the Government of Australia went missing in a Nov 2025 cyber incident; 6 of 22 structural benchmarks were not met by their end-Feb 2026 deadlines. A separate US$206mn Rapid Financing Instrument (SDR 150.5mn) was approved 19 Dec 2025 for cyclone relief. IMF staff visit 24-30 Jun 2026: 2026 growth cut to ~3% (from 5% in 2025) on the Middle East war; temporary fuel/electricity/fertilizer subsidies and cash transfers tolerated but the Fund insists on cost-recovery energy pricing, phasing out BoP measures, and exchange-rate flexibility. The 2026 primary-surplus target was lowered to 1.4% of GDP (from 2.3%) to accommodate cyclone recovery. The 7th review mission is expected later in 2026 (dates to be announced; reported as ~September). No successor-arrangement announcement found in fetched sources (null).

latestInflation.notes: CCPI (CBSL release 30 Jun 2026): headline 6.8% y/y in Jun 2026 (vs 5.5% May, 1.6% Feb) - approaching the top of CBSL's 5%+/-2pp band; +2.1% m/m; core 4.0% (from 3.9% May), non-food 8.4% (from 7.8%), food 3.6% (from 0.9%). CBSL attributes the acceleration to the pass-through of continued upward adjustments to domestic energy prices due to the Middle East conflict (Sri Lanka imports almost all of its oil), not domestic money-printing. The disinflation of 2023-25 is being tested by an exogenous energy shock. July 2026 CCPI not yet released as of this asOf.

LKA.fiscalNote: 2025 outturn: central-government overall deficit ~2.3% of GDP with a primary surplus of 5.4% of GDP - more than double the IMF's 2.3% floor, though the IMF flags the beat as largely one-off (a surge in motor-vehicle imports lifted revenue). All end-Dec-2025 fiscal QPCs met. For 2026 the IMF-endorsed program lowered the primary-surplus target to 1.4% of GDP (from 2.3%) and allows a central-government deficit up to ~5.1% of GDP, reflecting Cyclone Ditwah reconstruction plus temporary Middle East-war fuel/electricity/fertilizer subsidies and cash transfers - an acknowledged easing year. The 2026 budget (presented 7 Nov 2025, IMF-aligned) originally targeted a 2.5% primary surplus; the program reset it to 1.4% at the 5th+6th review. The primary balance remains in surplus; the trajectory is temporarily looser, not flipping to a primary deficit. The tell is the fall-2026 7th review - whether energy pricing keeps cost-recovery and whether the medium-term primary-surplus path is restored.
developments: IMF Executive Board completed the combined 5th+6th EFF reviews on 27 May 2026, releasing SDR 508mn (~US$695mn) and lifting total purchases to ~US$2.4bn; all end-Dec-2025 QPCs met, one waiver granted for a US$2.5mn Australia debt payment lost to a Nov 2025 cyber incident, and 6 of 22 structural benchmarks missed their end-Feb deadlines; debt-sustainability risk rated HIGH across all horizons [source: https://www.lankaweb.com/news/items/2026/07/05/passed-but-high-risk-what-the-imfs-5th-and-6th-review-actually-says-2/]

IMF staff visit (24-30 Jun 2026) met President Dissanayake, praised Sri Lanka's progress but cut 2026 growth to ~3% (from 5% in 2025), warned reserve accumulation has slowed, demanded cost-recovery energy pricing and exchange-rate flexibility, and set the 7th review mission for later in 2026 (reported ~September) [source: https://www.newswire.lk/2026/06/30/imf-reveals-whats-next-for-sri-lankas-bailout-programme/] [Verifier: the Newswire URL confirms the 24-30 Jun 2026 staff visit, the cost-recovery pricing emphasis, slowed reserve accumulation, and 7th review 'later this year, dates TBA', but does NOT itself state the 3% growth cut or exchange-rate-flexibility language; those are independently corroborated by the IMF PR 26/172 and EconomyNext 274069, both cited in sources.]

Middle East war / Strait of Hormuz disruption blew out the almost-entirely-imported energy bill: the monthly fuel import bill jumped to US$886mn in April 2026 from US$152mn in December 2025; administered fuel prices were raised four times since February (cumulatively ~38-46%), and the government rolled out temporary on-budget fuel/electricity/fertilizer subsidies plus cash transfers [source: https://economynext.com/explainer-what-does-the-latest-imf-review-mean-for-sri-lankans-274069/]

Inflation surged from 1.6% y/y (Feb 2026) to 6.8% y/y (Jun 2026) on energy pass-through, nearing CBSL's ~7% band ceiling; +2.1% m/m, core 4.0%, food 3.6%, non-food 8.4% [source: https://www.cbsl.gov.lk/en/news/ccpi-inflation-june-2026]

IMF explicitly cautioned the government against administrative manipulation or artificial propping up of the rupee, saying the exchange rate must act as the primary shock absorber - independently reinforcing the May 2026 allegation that CBSL had been informally capping USD/LKR near a 329-335 band [source: https://www.lankaweb.com/news/items/2026/05/24/central-bank-unofficially-caps-dollar-rate-to-curb-rupee-depreciation-says-rajith-keerthi-thennakoon/]

Cyclone Ditwah (28 Nov 2025) caused mass casualties and displacement; IMF approved a US$206mn RFI (SDR 150.5mn) on 19 Dec 2025 and deferred the 5th EFF review; reconstruction drives much of the 2026 fiscal easing [source: https://economynext.com/explainer-what-does-the-latest-imf-review-mean-for-sri-lankans-274069/]

killCriteriaWatch: FORCED DEVAL / PARALLEL GAP REOPENING - CLOSEST TO TRIPPING: LKR down ~11.5% y/y, reserves only US$6.8bn at end-2025 (~3.1 months of imports), a credible May-2026 allegation of an unofficial CBSL cap near 329-335, and explicit June-2026 IMF pushback that the exchange rate must be the primary shock absorber with no administrative propping-up. If the cap hardens while oil stays high, a kerb premium and import backlog would reopen the 2022 playbook. Not tripped: market still unified, rupee actually firmed ~0.67% over the past month.

INFLATION / COUNTRY RISK - WATCH: CCPI 6.8% y/y is near the top of CBSL's 5+/-2% band and rising (2.1% m/m in June); the driver is exogenous (imported energy) not monetary. IMF rates debt-sustainability risk HIGH across all horizons with debt above 100% of GDP. A second-round wage-price spiral or sustained high oil would widen the current account and re-rate risk. No verified USD spread series to monitor - spreadBps null; get Bloomberg/EMBI access.

FISCAL SLIPPAGE - PARTIAL/WATCH: 2026 is an acknowledged easing year - the IMF itself lowered the primary-surplus target to 1.4% of GDP (from 2.3%) for cyclone recovery, and 6 of 22 structural benchmarks slipped their end-Feb deadlines. The primary balance is still in surplus, but the fall-2026 7th review is the tell: if cost-recovery energy pricing is abandoned or the medium-term primary-surplus path is not restored, the fiscal leg of the thesis weakens. The 2025 primary beat (5.4%) was largely a one-off vehicle-import windfall, so do not extrapolate it.

REFORMER LOSING POWER - NOT CLOSE MECHANICALLY, ERODING POLITICALLY: the 159/225 supermajority runs to ~2029/2030 with no imminent national election, and Dissanayake is verified actively governing as of 8 Jul 2026. But NPP vote share collapsed ~18pp within eight months of taking office (May 2025 locals), and fuel-price hikes plus rising cost of living are stoking frustration; the risk is not losing office but buying popularity with IMF-breaking giveaways.

DMO issued an EOI on 29 Jun 2026 for eurobond transaction advisers (proposals due 13 Jul 2026), explicitly citing the S&P upgrade - Nigeria's first international issuance since the Nov 2025 $2.35bn sale (5x oversubscribed), confirming open market access at decade-low yields [source: http://nairametrics.com/2026/06/29/eurobond-fg-seeks-advisers-for-nigerias-global-debt-market-return/]

Sovereign eurobond yields fell to ~6.8% in early July 2026 (2026 low, down ~16bps week-on-week) on renewed offshore demand and firmer oil, re-rallying past the June Middle East yield spike to ~7.43% [source: https://businessday.ng/business-economy/article/nigeria-eurobond-yields-fall-to-6-78-on-strong-demand/]

S&P upgraded Nigeria to B/stable on 15 May 2026 - its first upgrade in 14 years - completing a three-agency upgrade sweep (Fitch B Apr 2025, affirmed Apr 2026; Moody's B3 May 2025); no NEW rating action has occurred since [source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-15/s-p-raises-nigeria-s-rating-for-first-time-in-14-years]

IMF concluded the 2026 Article IV on 1 June 2026 (surveillance only, no program): growth 4.0% (2025)/4.1% (2026), reforms praised, but IMF urged a neutral 2026 fiscal stance and disclosed ~N8.83tn (~2% of GDP) of 2025 spending omitted from official budget records [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/06/09/pr26190-nigeria-imf-executive-board-concludes-2026-article-iv-consultation-with-nigeria]

Election-year fiscal expansion re-confirmed: 2026 budget N68.32tn with a N31.46tn deficit, borrowing plan raised to N29.2tn; analysts warn debt-service-to-revenue is projected ~45% in 2026 and ~53% in 2027, flagging debt-trap risk into and past the Jan 2027 vote [source: https://www.legit.ng/business-economy/economy/1704230-fg-raises-2026-borrowing-plan-n292tn-budget-deficit-widens/]

INEC set the presidential/National Assembly election for 16 January 2027 (governorship/state-assembly 6 Feb 2027); Tinubu is APC's candidate with campaigns starting 19 Aug 2026 and the opposition still fragmented after the May 2026 ADC coalition collapse [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2027_Nigerian_general_election]
killCriteriaWatch: FISCAL (closest to tripping, re-confirmed): the budget never reached surplus and is deteriorating into the 16 Jan 2027 election - 2026 deficit N31.46tn (~4.4% of GDP, expansionary vs IMF's neutral-stance advice), borrowing plan raised 63% to N29.2tn, debt-service-to-revenue projected ~45% (2026)/~53% (2027), and the IMF revealed ~N8.83tn (~2% of GDP) of unreported spending, meaning true deficits exceed headline. This is the primary thesis risk.

PARALLEL FX (newly WIDENING, moved from 'not tripping' to WATCH): after appreciating through H1-2026, the naira reversed to depreciation in early July - official N1,375.75 and parallel N1,415 on 7 Jul push the gap to ~2.85% (vs the ~1.4% 30-Jun snapshot). Still functionally unified and far from the pre-2023 >60% gap, but the direction flipped and both rates are weakening; if the gap keeps widening on election-year FX demand, this channel reopens.

INFLATION/DISINFLATION STALL (watch): YoY inflation rose three consecutive months (15.06% Feb -> 15.93% May 2026) and m/m of 1.75-2.13% annualizes >20%; the unreleased June print carries a 16.0% consensus, implying a possible fourth straight rise. If election spending plus a softer naira push it back over ~20%, the re-rating narrative breaks. June NBS release due ~15 Jul 2026.

REFORMER POWER (moderate, rising into 16 Jan 2027 but NOT tripped): Tinubu remains in power, is APC's candidate, and the opposition is fragmented (ADC coalition collapsed May 2026), so near-term continuity is favored - but the transmission channel of election risk is the spending spree already visible in the 2026 budget, and campaigns begin 19 Aug 2026.

NOT tripping: country risk (eurobond yields at 2026 lows ~6.8%, three rating upgrades in 13 months with no adverse action since, DMO reopening market access) and reformer displacement (no coup/resignation/death; Tinubu governing normally).

reformer.role: Finance Minister since 11 March 2024; ex-JPMorgan/Habib Bank CEO technocrat; PM Shehbaz Sharif (PML-N-led coalition) re-elected 3 March 2024
reformer.nextElection: By 28 February 2029 (National Assembly elected 8 Feb 2024, 5-year term)
reformer.notes: Confirmed still in office and active as of 8 Jul 2026: Aurangzeb was addressing the Pakistan Banking Summit 2026 on 8 Jul 2026 and the Faisalabad Chamber on 3 Jul 2026, and announced a medium-term tax strategy, an SME Finance Task Force, and RFPs for new international debt issuance. Orthodox IMF-anchored technocrat program still over-delivering on fiscal: 9M-FY26 deficit 0.7% of GDP (lowest ever for the period), primary surplus 3.2%, three rating upgrades in 2025, Eurobond market re-entry Apr 2026, FBR/AI tax reform and a separate Tax Policy Office (Feb 2026), next-phase privatization of DISCOs/GENCOs/airports. Not Milei-grade: no popular mandate for shock therapy - reform rests on a contested 2024 election, jailed opposition leader Imran Khan, and army backing (27th constitutional amendment Nov 2025 expanded army chief powers and immunity, sparked PTI/TTAP nationwide protests, including a Feb 2026 national strike). Fuel-price street anger since the US-Israel-Iran war remains the main near-term political threat; the election itself is far off (2029). Score held at 58: orthodox and executing well (above 50) but constrained by a hybrid civil-military regime and lack of electoral mandate (well below shock-therapy grade).

parallelFx.notes: As of 8 Jul 2026: USD/PKR interbank around 278.66-279.0 and open-market around 278.95-279.0 (tradingeconomics mid-market ~279 on 8 Jul; forex.pk/pakbiz open-market ~278.95) - effectively zero regulated gap. The interbank/open-market split was closed by the Sept 2023 ISI/FIA crackdown on hawala/hundi networks and has stayed near zero through the 2026 oil shock; PKR is roughly flat vs ~279 a year ago (managed float under the IMF EFF, not a hard peg, and no step-devaluation in the last 12 months). Caveat: an UNREGULATED grey/hawala tier persists as a recognized channel, but no verified July-2026 grey-market quote was fetchable this run - a ~5% hawala premium reported in mid-2025 is treated as stale/unverified rather than current.
