countryRisk.notes: No free secondary-market EMBI/T-spread level was fetchable this run (Cbonds Pakistan index page and worldgovernmentbonds did not yield a usable figure), so spreadBps is set to null rather than invented. Reference points instead: the April 2026 Eurobond priced at 6.975% for a 3-year (upsized to $750m via greenshoe on strong demand), and the domestic 10-year government bond yield eased to ~11.69% on 6 Jul 2026. Trend is clearly falling: from market shut-out and near-default pricing in 2022-23 to three agency upgrades in 2025, restored market access at single-digit USD yields in Apr 2026, and a fresh July-2026 push to issue Eurobonds/Sukuk/dollar-settled rupee-linked bonds at what SBP Governor Jameel Ahmad (3 Jul 2026) said should be more favorable pricing. No ratings change since the prior asOf: Fitch AFFIRMED B- stable in April 2026 (not an upgrade), citing IMF-anchored fiscal consolidation and rebuilt FX buffers, with high energy-price exposure flagged as the key risk. No evidence of a post-war spread blowout.

PAK.imfProgram: Active and on track. 37-month EFF of ~$7.0bn (approved 25 Sep 2024) plus 28-month RSF of ~$1.4bn (approved 9 May 2025) - combined ~$8.4bn. IMF Executive Board completed the THIRD EFF review and SECOND RSF review on 8 May 2026, allowing drawings of ~$1.1bn (SDR 760m) under the EFF and ~$220m (SDR 154m) under the RSF, i.e. ~$1.32bn, bringing cumulative disbursements to ~$4.8bn. Staff-level agreement for that review was reached 27 Mar 2026. IMF cited strong fiscal performance (FY26 primary surplus 1.6% of GDP expected, in line with targets), inflation contained at that time, a broadly balanced current account, and reserves rebuilding (gross reserves $16bn end-Dec 2025 vs $14.5bn end-Jun 2025), while DMD Nigel Clarke noted a more challenging and uncertain external environment. IMF rejected the government's April 2026 request for larger fuel subsidies during the oil-price shock. No off-track signal or waiver flagged as of 8 Jul 2026; next (fourth) review not yet scheduled in fetched sources.

latestInflation.notes: CPI +11.1% YoY in June 2026 (urban 11.2%, rural 10.9%), -0.3% m/m (reversing a +0.5% m/m in May). Down from 11.7% in May 2026 but massively re-accelerated from 3.2% in June 2025 - far above SBP's 5-7% target. Full-year FY2025-26 average inflation was ~7.05%. Driver is exogenous: the US-Israel-Iran war pushed energy and transport costs sharply higher; food re-accelerated on 2025 flood damage. SBP hiked 100bps to 11.5% on 27 Apr 2026 (first hike since Jun 2023) and held on 15 Jun 2026, expecting double-digit inflation to persist near-term. Trend marked 'rising' on the 12-month trajectory (3.2%->11.1%) despite the one-month m/m easing in June. June was the latest monthly print available as of 8 Jul 2026 (July data not yet released).

PAK.fiscalNote: Strongly improving - the standout leg of the thesis. 9M-FY26 (Jul 2025-Mar 2026): overall fiscal deficit 0.7% of GDP (vs 2.6% a year earlier), first time in Pakistan's history below 1% for the period; primary surplus 3.2% of GDP (vs 3.0% 9M-FY25). Full-year FY26: primary surplus of 1.6% of GDP expected, in line with IMF targets; Moody's (Aug 2025) expected the FY26 overall deficit to narrow to 4.5-5% of GDP from 5.4% in FY25. FY2026-27 budget approved by parliament 23 Jun 2026: Rs18.77tn ($67.5bn) outlay, deficit target 3.6% of GDP, growth 4%, inflation 8.2% assumed - but defense spending up ~18%, and war-driven fuel-subsidy pressure is the key slippage risk. Tax administration is being tightened (AI/data-driven FBR, sugar-sector digital monitoring alone reportedly added ~Rs60bn in sales tax), and a medium-term tax strategy is promised within six months.
developments: IMF Executive Board completed the 3rd EFF / 2nd RSF reviews on 8 May 2026, allowing drawings of ~$1.1bn (SDR 760m) under the EFF and ~$220m (SDR 154m) under the RSF (~$1.32bn), bringing cumulative disbursements to ~$4.8bn of the ~$8.4bn combined program; FY26 primary surplus 1.6% of GDP expected, gross reserves $16bn end-Dec 2025 [source: https://www.nation.com.pk/10-May-2026/pakistan-s-strong-program-implementation-eff-arrangement-continues-imf]

On 8 July 2026 FM Aurangzeb (at the Pakistan Banking Summit 2026) announced Pakistan has issued RFPs to raise new financing via Eurobonds, Shariah-compliant Sukuk, and - for the first time - dollar-settled rupee-linked bonds, framed as refinancing to extend maturities rather than expand total external debt [source: https://theasianmirror.com/latest/81003/pakistan-plans-eurobonds-sukuk-and-dollar-settled-rupee-bonds/]

SBP Governor Jameel Ahmad said on 3 July 2026 that Pakistan plans to tap the Eurobond market at more favorable pricing as its external position improves; SBP-held reserves stood at $16.53bn (jump of $611m) and total liquid reserves at ~$22.04bn as of 24 June 2026 [source: https://profit.pakistantoday.com.pk/2026/04/28/sbp-expects-forex-reserves-to-exceed-18-billion-by-june/]

Pakistan returned to international capital markets in April 2026 with a 3-year Eurobond upsized to $750m (greenshoe exercised on strong demand) at 6.975% - its first international bond since 2022 [source: https://profit.pakistantoday.com.pk/2026/04/22/pakistan-invites-global-bids-for-eurobonds-sukuk-gmtn-transactions-over-next-three-years/]

Inflation stayed double-digit: 11.1% YoY in June 2026 (urban 11.2%, rural 10.9%, -0.3% m/m), down from 11.7% in May but up from 3.2% in June 2025, far above SBP's 5-7% target; FY26 average ~7.05% [source: https://www.brecorder.com/news/40428026/pakistan-inflation-hits-111-in-june-2026]

SBP hiked the policy rate 100bps to 11.5% on 27 April 2026 - the first hike since June 2023 - and held at 11.5% on 15 June 2026, citing war-driven fuel prices and fiscal-slippage risk [source: https://tradingeconomics.com/pakistan/interest-rate]

Fitch AFFIRMED Pakistan at 'B-' with a stable outlook in April 2026 (not an upgrade), citing IMF-aligned fiscal consolidation and rebuilt FX buffers, flagging high energy-price exposure as the key risk and noting Pakistan's regional 'ceasefire broker' role could partly offset external pressures [source: https://www.dawn.com/news/1991496/fitch-affirms-pakistans-rating-at-b-with-stable-outlook]

US-Israel-Iran war oil shock remains the dominant macro drag: it drove the inflation re-acceleration and the SBP's first hike in nearly three years, and the IMF rejected Pakistan's April request for bigger fuel subsidies [source: https://www.aljazeera.com/economy/2026/4/30/soaring-fuel-prices-in-pakistan-threaten-economic-and-political-crises]
killCriteriaWatch: Inflation leg of the thesis has partially REVERSED (closest to tripping): CPI went from 3.2% YoY (Jun 2025) to 11.1% (Jun 2026) on the war oil shock, forcing SBP's first rate hike since 2023 - exogenous rather than policy failure, but 'crushed inflation' no longer holds; watch whether it decelerates toward the budget's 8.2% FY27 assumption (June's -0.3% m/m is a tentative first sign of easing)

Fiscal slippage risk building but not tripped: IMF-rejected fuel-subsidy demands, ~18% defense spending increase, and 2025 flood costs all pressure the FY27 3.6% deficit target; the primary surplus (3.2% of GDP 9M-FY26) still provides a large buffer, and the FY26 outturn remains historically strong

Reformer/political risk elevated but not tripped: reformer confirmed in office (Aurangzeb active 8 Jul 2026); fuel-price street anger, jailed Imran Khan and PTI/TTAP mobilization (Feb 2026 national strike) against the Nov 2025 27th amendment persist; the program's survival rests on army backing rather than a popular mandate - durable until the 2029 election but brittle to unrest

Parallel-gap reopening: not tripped - interbank and open-market rates are at par (~279) as of 8 Jul 2026 - but the unregulated hawala tier and a sustained elevated oil import bill remain the triggers to watch; no verified July-2026 grey-market premium was found

Country-risk blowout: NOT close to tripping - three upgrades in 2025, Fitch B- affirmation in Apr 2026, market re-entry at 6.975% for the April $750m Eurobond, and a fresh July-2026 issuance push at expected better pricing; no evidence of post-shock spread widening in sources found (though no live secondary spread was fetchable, so this is a qualitative read)

reformer.notes: Orthodox but constrained. Simsek's mid-2023 program (tight money, fiscal discipline, 3-stage roadmap in its 'final/structural transformation' phase) is genuine and IMF-endorsed: inflation down from 75% (May 2024) to 32.11% (June 2026), Moody's/Fitch/S&P all upgraded or raised outlooks since mid-2025. But he has no mandate of his own - the program exists at Erdogan's pleasure. 2026 shocks forced retreat: CBRT raised its 2026 inflation target from 16% to 24% (May 14, 2026), fiscal slipped in Apr-May 2026, and Erdogan's judicial crackdown on the opposition (Imamoglu jailed since Mar 2025, shuttled across three court hearings in one day just before the late-June NATO summit; CHP congress annulled and Kilicdaroglu reinstated May 2026) repeatedly hits markets the program must then stabilize. Not Milei-grade: no free float (managed crawl defended with reserves), no fiscal surplus, official CPI disputed. Marginally more constructive than the prior asOf: CDS narrowed to ~223bps in early July and reserves were rebuilt to ~$164bn by mid-July, so score nudged 55->56. [Verifier 2026-07-08: independently re-checked the 5 load-bearing claims and all confirmed. (1) Simsek still Treasury/Finance Minister and Karahan CBRT governor (multiple July 2026 sources); election due by 2028. (2) June 2026 CPI 32.11% y/y confirmed via TradingEconomics/Reuters, m/m +0.99%. (3) 5Y CDS ~223bps confirmed; Moody's Ba3 stable (upgraded Jul 25, 2025), Fitch BB- positive (Jan 24, 2026), S&P BB-/B stable all confirmed. (4) USDTRY 46.8724 and all-time-high 46.93 in July 2026 confirmed on TradingEconomics; no parallel market. (5) IMF surveillance-only, 2025 Article IV concluded Feb 13, 2026 confirmed. May 2026 budget deficit TRY 298.2bn also confirmed. Corrected top-level asOf from 2026-07-09 to 2026-07-08 to match verification date; internal 'Re-verified 2026-07-09' note-tags reflect the data window and remain accurate. No em dashes present.]

parallelFx.notes: No meaningful parallel/black-market rate: the lira is convertible and trades onshore/offshore at a single rate under a heavily managed 'controlled crawl' float. USDTRY 46.8724 on 2026-07-09 (Trading Economics); the pair set a fresh all-time high of 46.93 in July 2026, extending a gradual managed depreciation; lira roughly -17% y/y (less than inflation, i.e., real appreciation continues). No hard capital controls, but heavy state management: after the Feb 28, 2026 Iran-war shock the CBRT sold/swapped tens of billions of USD and gold to hold the line, then intervened again in FX and suspended one-week repo auctions after the May 2026 CHP court ruling (lifting the interbank overnight rate ~300bps toward 40%). Reserves have since been rebuilt to about $164.4bn by mid-July 2026 per ING, though ING flags the gains as fragile. A dual-rate episode was last a risk in 2023, not currently. Parallel rate field null because none is quoted.

latestInflation.notes: Official TurkStat: 32.11% y/y (vs 32.61% May), lowest since March 2026; m/m +0.99% (vs +1.71% May), smallest gain in six months; core (ex energy, food, beverages, tobacco, gold) eased to 29.8% from 30.4%. Simsek: 'the disinflation process, which had been interrupted by rising global energy prices due to supply shocks, has resumed.' Path was non-monotonic in 2026: ~30.9% Dec 2025, re-accelerated to ~32.4% by April on the Middle East war energy shock, now easing again. CBRT year-end 2026 forecast 26% (revised up from 15-21% range); interim target raised from 16% to 24% on May 14, 2026. Policy rate held at 37% at the June 2026 MPC (third straight hold; corridor 35.5-40%); next MPC is Jul 23, 2026, not yet held. Credibility caveat: independent ENAG group estimated 51.4% y/y for June per the prior asOf reporting (not re-fetched this run). No newer print exists as of 2026-07-09 (July CPI is due early August).

TUR.fiscalNote: Deficit, worsening at the margin. 2025 consolidated deficit ~3.1% of GDP; 2024 was 4.9%. 2026 MTP (in force since Sep 7, 2025) targets a 3.5%-of-GDP deficit, which Simsek insists is 'well within reach'. But May 2026 swung to a TRY 298bn ($6.4bn) deficit vs a year-ago surplus - revenue -18% y/y (income, corporate and VAT all down, plus a March special-consumption-tax suspension to ease fuel prices), spending +27% y/y, and the five-month cumulative deficit hit TRY 1.1 trillion, well over half the TRY 1.93 trillion full-year target. No June print yet as of 2026-07-09 (search hits labelled 'June' were restatements of the May release). No surplus in sight - the 'kill the deficit' leg of the thesis is absent; this is gradual consolidation, currently slipping.

killCriteriaWatch: Forced devaluation / FX pressure - NEAR but EASING: lira set a fresh all-time high (46.93) in July and the crawl is a defended one, but the acute stress has faded since May - CDS tightened to ~223bps and reserves rebuilt to ~$164bn by mid-July. Still geopolitics-dependent and, per ING, 'fragile'. No parallel gap.

Reformer losing power - MEDIUM: Simsek and Karahan remain in place with Erdogan's backing and Simsek calls early-election risk 'very low', but the program is hostage to Erdogan's political project - the opposition crackdown (Imamoglu jailed and paraded through serial hearings, CHP leadership annulled) keeps shocking markets, and third-term/early-election maneuvering raises odds of a populist spending pivot into any election.

Budget flipping back to deficit - PARTIALLY TRIPPED in spirit: Turkey never reached surplus (thesis leg absent); May 2026 posted a TRY 298bn deficit vs a year-ago surplus, revenue -18% y/y, and the 5-month deficit is already over half the full-year target. Watch whether the 3.5%-of-GDP 2026 target survives; June data not yet out.

Country risk blowing out - NOT TRIPPED and improving: 5Y CDS ~223bps, tighter than the ~242bps mid-June spike and back toward the sub-210 lows of early 2026; all three agencies hold or improve ratings. Watch Moody's Jul 24, 2026 review (still pending).

Disinflation stalling (thesis-adjacent): CBRT already raised its 2026 target 16%->24% and paused cuts, but the June print (32.11%, core 29.8%) shows disinflation resuming after the spring energy shock. Official CPI still far above target and the independent ENAG series remains far higher, questioning the official numbers.

reformer.name: Volodymyr Zelensky (President), with PM Yulia Svyrydenko and Finance Minister Serhii Marchenko running the IMF/EU-anchored economic program
reformer.role: President; PM Svyrydenko and FinMin Marchenko lead fiscal/reform execution; NBU runs orthodox monetary policy
reformer.nextElection: None scheduled - presidential/parliamentary elections barred under martial law, which Zelensky signed extended from 4 May 2026 for 90 days (the 19th extension) until 2 Aug 2026. A Verkhovna Rada working group on wartime-election legislation held its first session 26 Dec 2025; Zelensky said on 9 Dec 2025 he could hold a vote in 60-90 days if the US and European allies guarantee security. As of 9 Jul 2026 no election is set and none can occur while martial law stands.
reformer.notes: This is orthodox technocratic management under an IMF anchor, not Milei-style shock therapy: NBU keeps a tight 15% policy rate and near-unified managed-float FX, and the 48-month EFF (approved Feb 2026) carries revenue-mobilization and governance conditionality (VAT parcel exemption removal, transfer-pricing, simplified-tax-regime reform). But there is no fiscal consolidation - the 2026 budget plans an 18.4%-of-GDP war deficit financed by foreign aid - and reform/anticorruption credibility has continued to deteriorate. The Nov 2025 Energoatom/Midas scheme ($100M kickback operation exposed by NABU/SAPO) has since escalated: NABU and SAPO formally charged Andriy Yermak, Zelensky's former chief of staff, with money laundering (~460M UAH / ~$9-10.5M via a luxury-housing project) - reported 11 May 2026. Score lowered to 37 (from 40): credible orthodox team and IMF/EU anchor remain, but the deepening corruption probe reaching the president's inner circle, the war-constrained mandate, and a missed VAT structural benchmark under the new EFF all weigh on reform credibility. [Verifier 2026-07-09: independently re-checked the 5 load-bearing claims via fresh searches. Confirmed: (1) Zelensky in power, no election scheduled, martial law extended to 2 Aug 2026 (19th extension, signed ~30 Apr 2026); (2) May 2026 CPI 8.2% YoY / +0.9% m/m, core 7.9%, NBU rate held 15% on 18 Jun; (3) ratings S&P CCC+ (from SD 22 Jan 2026), Fitch CCC (from RD Dec 2025), Moody's Ca stable, no new action through 9 Jul; (4) FX official 44.4261 UAH/USD on 9 Jul (TradingEconomics), -5.90% 12mo, cash-vs-official gap ~1.7%; (5) IMF first-review SLA 12 Jun (~$690M/SDR 503M), Board NOT yet approved, meeting scheduled ~20 Jul, one VAT benchmark missed. Yermak money-laundering charge (~460M UAH, 11 May 2026) and Kyiv Independent URL both confirmed. No missed material development (no default, coup, resignation, rating cut, or election). No em dashes. asOf advanced to 2026-07-09 to match current date.]

parallelFx.notes: No classic black market - the hryvnia moved from a hard peg to NBU 'managed flexibility' in Oct 2023 and the cash market is legal - but martial-law FX controls remain and a persistent cash-vs-official gap has held since mid-June 2026. Per KYT Group (report dated 30 Jun 2026), the official rate was 44.85 UAH/USD at end-June while bank/exchange-office cash SELLING rates ran 45.05-45.30 for over two weeks (I use the 45.18 midpoint as the parallel proxy). Against the newer official rate of 44.4261 (TradingEconomics, 9 Jul 2026, down 5.90% over 12 months) that cash band implies a gap of roughly 1.4-1.9% (I use ~1.7%); against end-June's 44.85 the gap was ~0.4-1.0%. NBU is defending the corridor with heavy intervention: $4.63bn of FX sold in June vs $3.03bn in May. UKR.imfProgram: Active but first review NOT YET board-approved as of 9 Jul 2026. New 48-month Extended Fund Facility of ~US$8.1bn approved by the IMF Executive Board on 26 Feb 2026 (SLA 26 Nov 2025; replaced/cancelled the 2023 EFF whose 8th review completed Jun 2025). Staff-level agreement on the FIRST review plus 2026 Article IV was reached 12 Jun 2026: ~US$690M (SDR 503M) upon Board approval, which would bring total disbursements under the new program to ~US$2.2bn. All end-March quantitative performance criteria and indicative targets were met, but two Q1 structural benchmarks were implemented with delay and ONE (a VAT benchmark) was MISSED; IMF staff greenlit the tranche anyway. IMF spokesperson Julie Kozack (25 Jun) said the Board would consider the review 'in the coming weeks'; press reports flag a likely Board date around 20 Jul 2026. IMF says the program remains fully financed on continued large-scale external support; 2026 GDP growth projected at only ~1.0-1.6%.

latestInflation.notes: Latest print is still May 2026 as of 9 Jul 2026: 8.2% YoY (down from 8.6% in Apr), m/m index 100.9 (i.e. +0.9% m/m); core 7.9% YoY (per NBU, core accelerated slightly). minfin.com.ua explicitly states June 2026 data has NOT yet been published by Derzhstat. Disinflation continuing but above the NBU's 5% target. NBU held its key rate at 15% on 18 Jun 2026 (effective 19 Jun; third straight hold after a 50bp cut from 15.5% in Jan 2026) and stands ready to hike; next rate decision is 30 Jul 2026.

UKR.fiscalNote: Massive structural war deficit - this thesis leg is absent. The 2026 budget plans a deficit of ~UAH 1.9trn, 18.4% of GDP (~$41.5bn per Reuters; ~$45bn per OSW at different FX), with defense/security spending alone at UAH 2.8trn (~27.2% of GDP). Revenues ~UAH 2.9trn vs expenditures ~UAH 4.8trn. Ministry of Finance estimated a EUR 16bn unfunded gap for 2026 and a $60.8bn external financing need for 2026-27 (IMF estimate ~$63bn); OSW judges the budget 'unrealistic' (defense underfunded, civilian spending 100% dependent on foreign money). Financing was largely secured by the 19 Dec 2025 EU deal (EUR 90bn/$105bn for 2026-27 via joint EU borrowing). The IMF program pushes revenue mobilization (VAT parcel exemption removal, transfer-pricing, simplified-tax-regime curbs) - though the VAT structural benchmark was missed at the Q1 checkpoint. Trajectory: deficits of this scale persist as long as the war does; NBU FX intervention ($4.63bn in June alone) is itself a drain financed by aid tranches.

War intensity undiminished: a Russian missile/drone barrage (77 missiles, 28 ballistic) killed at least 22 in Kyiv on 2 Jul 2026; a second large strike on 6 Jul 2026 (68 missiles, 351 drones) killed at least 20 (14 in Kyiv) on the eve of a NATO summit in Turkiye [source: https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/7/6/russian-attacks-on-ukraine-kill-11-on-eve-of-nato-summit-authorities-say]

killCriteriaWatch: Budget flipping back to deficit: ALREADY FAILED for this thesis - Ukraine never had a surplus; 2026 plans an 18.4%-of-GDP deficit wholly dependent on foreign financing, and OSW judges even that understated. The IMF's own VAT revenue-mobilization benchmark was MISSED at the Q1 checkpoint (Jun 2026).

Forced devaluation / parallel gap reopening: EARLY WARNING (widening) - cash-market selling rates held ~45.05-45.30 through late June/early July, ~1.4-1.9% above the 9 Jul official rate of 44.4261; NBU intervention burn jumped ~53% m/m to $4.63bn in June, and its own corridor tolerates drift to ~47 UAH/USD (year-end forecast ~46.5). A cut-off of external aid would force a sharp devaluation.

Reformer losing power: WATCH (elevated) - no elections possible under martial law (extended to 2 Aug 2026), but the corruption probe has now reached the president's former chief of staff Yermak (charged May 2026), damaging Zelensky's reform credibility; US pressure for a wartime vote persists, and any ceasefire triggers elections within months with an untested outcome.

Country risk blowing out: WATCH - restructured bonds are priced on peace hopes and remain a headline trade; peace talks stayed stuck through July 2026 (Donbas the sticking point) and two deadly July strikes underscored no de-escalation, so a definitive collapse of negotiations would reprice the whole curve. No fresh bps spread could be fetched this run.

reformer.name: Hakainde Hichilema
reformer.role: President (UPND), with Finance Minister Situmbeko Musokotwane executing the program
reformer.nextElection: 2026-08-13 general election (presidential + parliamentary); Hichilema seeking second term
reformer.notes: Businessman-president elected Aug 2021 on an orthodox reform platform; still in power and campaigning for re-election. Track record: completed 38-month IMF ECF (final review Jan 2026), restructured ~94% of external debt perimeter under G20 Common Framework, cut inflation from ~24% to 6.5%, ran a 3.1%-of-GDP primary surplus in 2025, rebuilt reserves to $6.4bn (4.4 months imports), improved Transparency Intl CPI rank 117th->92nd. Not Milei-grade shock therapy: orthodox IMF-anchored gradualism, and discipline is slipping pre-election (fuel VAT/excise suspension, ag subsidy overruns ~1.3% of GDP, civil-service wage hike). Political risk to program is LOW-MODERATE: opposition fragmented (14 approved candidates; main rival ex-president Lungu was disqualified by Constitutional Court and died June 2025); a University of Zambia-supervised poll (fieldwork 2-15 Oct 2025, n=1,009, +/-3.1%) put Hichilema at 60% vs 35% combined opposition, 58-63% in head-to-head matchups. He named VP Mutale Nalumango as running mate and filed nominations on 22 May 2026. Election is a binary event ~5 weeks out; IMF successor-program talks deferred until after the vote. Ministers (incl. Musokotwane) ceased to hold office when the National Assembly was dissolved 15 May 2026 and are campaigning; Hichilema remains President, so 'reformer in power' stands but the election is the live risk. Caveat: independent commentary and opposition figures argue macro stability has not reached households (food prices, load-shedding, youth unemployment), the gap the vote will test.

parallelFx.notes: Kwacha is free-floating and unified - parallel/black-market premium was a 1970s-1990s phenomenon (unified during early-1990s liberalization; academic sources on the premium are all historical). No current material parallel market found; gap is effectively zero. Official/interbank rate 18.15 ZMW/USD on 9 Jul 2026 (Trading Economics), fluctuating ~17.99-18.47 over late June-early July 2026. IMPORTANT SHIFT since prior asOf: the kwacha has begun REVERSING - it weakened ~3.5% over the past month (to ~18.15-18.47 from ~17.9-18.0 in late June) even though it is still up ~22-24% over the trailing 12 months on copper, reserves build and carry inflows. Reversal risk flagged in the prior note (28.9% of government securities held by non-residents / offshore carry trade, and fuel subsidies masking pass-through) is now visibly starting to materialize into a softer currency ahead of the election. [Verifier 2026-07-08: independently confirmed Trading Economics USD/ZMW 18.15 on Jul 9, -3.54% over the trailing month, +22.52% over 12 months. Note the IMF (May 2026) still characterized the kwacha as 'stronger' and Hichilema's Jul 7 Copperbelt campaign cites a 'strengthening kwacha'; the recent 1-month softening is real but modest and the trailing-12-month move is still strongly positive, so 'reversal beginning' is a watch-item framing, not a confirmed trend break.]

ZMB.imfProgram: 38-month Extended Credit Facility (approved Aug 31, 2022, extended Sep 2025) COMPLETED: IMF Executive Board finished the sixth and final review on Jan 27, 2026, disbursing SDR 138.9M (~US$190M), total ~SDR 1,271.66M (~US$1.7bn). Zambia abandoned a planned 1-year extension (Bloomberg, Jan 7, 2026) and let the program expire; a successor arrangement is under discussion but negotiations are expected to conclude with the incoming government AFTER the Aug 2026 elections (IMF, Mar 2026, said talks may stretch beyond the vote). The Apr 30-May 13, 2026 IMF staff visit (mission led by Edward Gemayel) praised inflation/reserves/debt progress but warned pre-election spending threatens fiscal gains, cutting the 2026 primary-surplus projection to 1.1% of GDP from 3.8% and flagging inflation rising to ~8.5% by end-2026. Zambia is currently WITHOUT an active IMF anchor; the government has publicly reaffirmed its commitment to a new IMF-supported, investment-focused program once the elections conclude.

latestInflation.asOf: June 2026 (ZamStats release; July 2026 print not yet published as of 2026-07-08)
latestInflation.notes: June 2026 remains the latest official print - ZamStats had not released the July 2026 figure as of 2026-07-08 (it publishes late in the month). YoY 6.5% in June (a fresh low since Feb 2018) vs 6.6% May, 6.8% April, 7.1% March, 11.2% Dec 2025 - inside the Bank of Zambia 6-8% target band, down from ~24% when Hichilema took office. M/m 0.1% in June vs 0.2% in May. Food 6.7%, non-food 6.0% YoY. Disinflation aided by a bumper maize harvest and (until recently) a firm kwacha. BoZ cut the policy rate three times in 2026 to 13.25% (May 13, 2026; -75bp Feb, -25bp May). Caveats: IMF (May 2026) projects inflation RISING to ~8.5% by end-2026 on election spending; independent analysis notes suspended fuel VAT/excise is masking ~K6-7/liter of pump-price pressure; and the kwacha has started to weaken, which could feed pass-through into H2 2026.

ZMB.fiscalNote: 2025 outturn: overall fiscal deficit ~4.6% of GDP but PRIMARY SURPLUS of 3.1% of GDP (IMF). 2026 budget (K253.1bn, 27.4% of GDP, tabled by Musokotwane) targets a 2.1% overall deficit, but trajectory is deteriorating into the August election: IMF (May 2026) cut its 2026 primary-surplus projection to 1.1% of GDP from 3.8%, citing fuel VAT/excise suspension, civil-service wage rise, and ~1.3%-of-GDP agricultural subsidy overruns. Independent analysis (Canary Compass, May 13, 2026): Q1 deficit consumed ~40% of the annual target in two months, a K26.3bn supplementary budget was tabled Apr 30, and the overall 2026 deficit could reach ~4% of GDP - nearly double target. Public debt fell to ~87.6% of GDP in 2025 from 101.8% in 2024 (IMF); Fitch projects ~85% in 2026, aided by the June 2026 buyback that retired the $1.36bn 2053 eurobond at a discount (~$1.1bn repurchase price). Direction: consolidation achieved 2022-2025, now slipping in the election year, though the budget has NOT flipped back to a primary deficit.
developments: IMF Executive Board completed the sixth and FINAL review of Zambia's ECF on Jan 27, 2026, disbursing ~US$190M (total ~US$1.7bn); Zambia dropped its extension request and the program expired, leaving no IMF anchor until post-election successor talks [source: https://www.zambiainvest.com/economy/imf-extended-credit-facility-sixth-review/]

Fitch upgraded Zambia to B- (stable) from Restricted Default on Nov 28, 2025, and S&P to CCC+ (stable) from SD on Nov 21, 2025 - two upgrades in eight days ending five years in default, on ~94% completion of the external debt restructuring; NO new rating action since [source: https://www.investing.com/news/world-news/fitch-upgrades-zambia-to-b-with-stable-outlook-after-debt-restructuring-93CH-4382030]

June 2026: Zambia completed the 'world's first debt-for-energy swap' - a buyback of its $1.36bn face-value 2053 restructured eurobond for a ~$1.1bn repurchase price (tender clearing at 84.35c, falling toward 82.76c near full participation), part-funded by a $600M concessional AfDB loan; the ~$275M discount is committed to grid/electrification spending over 15 years and the 2053 bond traded ~82.61c on Jun 9 [source: https://www.ecofinagency.com/news-finances/1006-56345-zambia-s-debt-buyback-frees-up-275-million-for-electrification]

Inflation held at 6.5% YoY in June 2026 (m/m 0.1%), a fresh low since Feb 2018 and inside the BoZ 6-8% target band; the July 2026 print was not yet released as of Jul 8; BoZ has cut its policy rate to 13.25% (third cut, May 13, 2026) [source: https://www.zamstats.gov.zm/annual-inflation-june-2025-june-2026/]

IMF staff visit (Apr 30-May 13, 2026, mission led by Edward Gemayel) confirmed reserves at $6.4bn (4.4 months of imports) and restructuring at ~94% of perimeter, but cut the 2026 primary surplus to 1.1% of GDP from 3.8%, trimmed 2026 growth to 4.3% and saw inflation rising to ~8.5% by end-2026 on pre-election spending; both sides reaffirmed intent to negotiate a successor program after the vote [source: https://www.miningnewszambia.com/imf-warns-zambia-election-spending-fuel-costs-threaten-fiscal-gains/]

The kwacha has begun REVERSING its 2025-26 rally: it traded ~18.15/USD on Jul 9, 2026 and weakened ~3.5% over the trailing month even while still up ~22-24% over 12 months - an early sign the copper/carry-driven strength may be fading into the election [source: https://tradingeconomics.com/zambia/currency]

A University of Zambia-supervised opinion poll (fieldwork 2-15 Oct 2025, n=1,009, +/-3.1%) put Hichilema at 60% vs 35% combined opposition, 58-63% in head-to-head matchups; he named VP Mutale Nalumango as running mate and filed nominations for the Aug 13 election on May 22, 2026 [source: https://www.mwebantu.com/opinion-poll-shows-president-hakainde-hichilema-headed-for-victory-in-2026/]

Election Aug 13, 2026: 14 approved presidential candidates in a fragmented field; ex-president Lungu was disqualified by the Constitutional Court and died in June 2025; commentators note the vote is a referendum on whether macro stabilization has reached households, with food prices, load-shedding and youth unemployment the key pressure points [source: https://allafrica.com/stories/202607050002.html]
killCriteriaWatch: FISCAL SLIPPAGE (closest to tripping): primary surplus projected to collapse from 3.8% to 1.1% of GDP in 2026 (IMF, May 2026); Q1 deficit consumed ~40% of the annual target in two months, a K26.3bn supplementary budget was tabled, fuel VAT/excise suspended, ag subsidies overran by ~1.3% of GDP, and the April domestic bond auction was only 43% subscribed - overall deficit could hit ~4% of GDP vs 2.1% target. Budget has NOT flipped to primary deficit yet, but the trend is wrong.

IMF ANCHOR REMOVED: ECF expired Jan 2026 with no successor until after the Aug 13 election - fiscal discipline is currently unenforced. Government has reaffirmed intent to negotiate a new program post-vote, but nothing is signed. Watch whether a new IMF arrangement is agreed in H2 2026.

REFORMER LOSING POWER: binary event Aug 13, 2026. Risk still looks low (Hichilema ~60% in the last public poll, fragmented 14-candidate opposition, Lungu dead/disqualified), but a surprise loss or disputed result would gut the thesis.

HOT-MONEY / FX REVERSAL RISK (now partially materializing - upgraded from early-warning): the kwacha has weakened ~3.5% in the past month after its big rally, with strength resting partly on a carry trade (non-residents hold 28.9% of government securities and supplied ~76% of Q1 net issuance). A post-election fiscal blowout or global risk-off could accelerate the reversal. No parallel-market gap exists to reopen; country risk is still compressing (no adverse rating action), not blowing out.

access.accessNotes: No US-listed Zambia-specific ETF and no US-listed Zambian single names. Broad-Africa funds (e.g. VanEck Africa Index ETF, AFK) exist and trade, but Zambia is not a meaningful weight and this is not Zambia access. Indirect route is copper miners with Zambian assets (e.g., First Quantum, OTC only). Effectively no US retail equity access; status unchanged this refresh.
